  - **Anterior olfactory nucleus**, **olfactory tubercle**, **amygdala** (innate valence), **entorhinal cortex** (memory association).
- Second-order projection from piriform → **orbitofrontal cortex (OFC)** via mediodorsal thalamus for conscious odor discrimination, and to **hippocampus** for olfactory memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board pearl:** Olfaction is the **only sensory modality** that reaches the cerebral cortex *without* an obligatory thalamic relay at the level of first cortical input. This is a very common **Part A (3-mark) question**.

---

## 2.2 The Gustatory System — Anatomical and Functional Architecture

### 2.2.1 Peripheral Gustatory Apparatus

- **Taste buds** are onion-shaped clusters of **50–100 taste receptor cells (TRCs)**, plus supporting and basal cells.
- **Taste pores** at the apical surface permit tastant access.
- **Lingual distribution:** Fungiform papillae (anterior 2/3 of tongue, innervated by **CN VII — chorda tympani**), circumvallate papillae (posterior 1/3, **CN IX**), foliate papillae (lateral, **CN IX**), and epiglottic/ pharyngeal taste buds (**CN X**).
- **Taste bud turnover:** ~ 10–14 days from basal cell precursors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic "tongue map" is a myth:** All five qualities can be sensed across the entire tongue, with only modest regional biases (sweet slightly anterior, bitter slightly posterior). The 1901 Hänig map has been disproven. Mentioning this in a KTU essay demonstrates current knowledge.

### 2.2.2 The Five Basic Taste Qualities and Their Molecular Receptors

| Quality | Stimulus | Receptor Type | Key Transduction Mechanism | Valence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sweet** | Sugars, sweeteners, sweet-tasting amino acids | T1R2 + T1R3 (heterodimeric GPCR, Class C) | α-gustducin → PLCβ2 → IP₃ → TRPM5 | Appetitive (positive) |
| **Umami** | L-glutamate, 5'-ribonucleotides (GMP, IMP) | T1R1 + T1R3 heterodimer (GPCR); also mGluR1, mGluR4 | Same G-protein cascade | Appetitive (positive) |
| **Bitter** | Alkaloids, plant toxins, cations | **T2R family** (~ 25 functional in human, GPCR Class A/F) | α-gustducin → PLCβ2 → IP₃ → TRPM5 | Aversive (negative) |
| **Salty** | Na⁺ (and other alkali cations) | **ENaC** (epithelial sodium channel, amiloride-sensitive); also non-amiloride pathways | Direct cation influx → depolarization | Appetitive (low conc.) / aversive (high conc.) |
| **Sour** | Acids (extracellular H⁺, low pH) | **OTOP1** (proton channel); PKD2L1 candidate marker | H⁺ influx via OTOP1 → depolarization | Aversive (typically) |

### 2.2.3 Type II (Receptor) Cell Cascade — Sweet/Umami/Bitter

$$
\text{Tastant} + \text{T1R/T2R} \;\to\; \alpha\text{-gustducin} \;\to\; \text{PLC}\beta_{2} \;\to\; \text{PIP}_{2} \;\to\; \text{IP}_{3} + \text{DAG} \;\to\; \text{IP}_{3}\text{R}_{3} \;\to\; \uparrow [\text{Ca}^{2+}]_{i} \;\to\; \text{TRPM5} \;\to\; \text{Na}^{+}\text{ influx} \;\to\; \text{Depolarization}
$$

Then the TRC releases **ATP** through **CALHM1/3 channels** → purinergic activation of **P2X2/P2X3 receptors** on afferent nerve fibers (CN VII, IX, X). This is **vesicular-independent** ATP release, a unique feature of taste cells.

### 2.2.4 Type I (Glial-like) and Type III (Presynaptic) Cells

- **Type I cells:** Glia-like, express GLAST (glutamate transporter); mediate salt taste partially.
- **Type III cells:** Express **SNAP-25**, form synapses with afferent fibers; mediate **sour** taste via **OTOP1** and PKD2L1; integrate via conventional vesicular neurotransmitter release (5-HT, GABA).

### 2.2.5 Central Gustatory Pathway

$$
\text{Taste Bud} \;\xrightarrow{\text{CN VII, IX, X}}\; \text{Rostral Nucleus of Solitary Tract (rNST)} \;\to\; \text{VPMpc (parvocellular VPM) thalamus} \;\to\; \text{Primary Gustatory Cortex (insula, frontal operculum)} \;\to\; \text{OFC}
$$

- **rNST** also projects to:
  - **Amygdala** (taste-valence learning),
  - **Hypothalamus** (lateral hypothalamus = feeding center, ventromedial = satiety),
  - **Brainstem reticular formation** (cephalic-phase reflexes: salivation, insulin release, gastric acid secretion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — comparison of central pathways:**
> - **Olfaction:** Bulb → Piriform (no thalamic relay at first cortical stage).
> - **Gustation:** NST → **VPMpc thalamus** (relay required) → Gustatory cortex.
> - **Vision/audition/somatosensation:** All have obligatory thalamic relays.
> Only olfaction is "thalamus-independent" at first cortical synapse.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Chemosensory Physiology

| # | Equation / Law | Description | Variables / Constants | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{R_{max} \cdot C}{K_{d} + C}$ | **Beidler (taste) equation** — steady-state response R vs. tastant concentration C | $R$ = response; $R_{max}$ = maximal response; $K_{d}$ = dissociation constant of tastant-receptor binding | C in mol/L; valid at equilibrium |
| 2 | $\theta = \dfrac{[L]^{n}}{K_{d} + [L]^{n}}$ | **Hill equation** — cooperative binding (used for odorant-receptor complexes with cooperativity n) | $n$ = Hill coefficient | n = 1: non-cooperative; n > 1: positive cooperativity |
| 3 | $V_{m} = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{o} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{o} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{i}}{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{i} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{i} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{o}}\right)$ | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation** — used to compute ORN/TRC receptor potential | $P$ = permeability; concentrations in M; $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; $F = 96485$ C·mol⁻¹; $T$ = 310 K | Body temperature |
| 4 | $\tau_{adapt} = \dfrac{1}{k_{off} + k_{degrade}}$ | Approximation of **chemosensory adaptation time constant** | $k_{off}$ = ligand off-rate; $k_{degrade}$ = second-messenger hydrolysis | Seconds |
| 5 | $\text{Threshold}_{odor} \approx 10^{-12}\,M$ for skatole | Empirical **olfactory detection threshold** (highly ligand-dependent) | Skatole reference: ~ $1.4 \times 10^{-12}$ M = 0.04 ppb | Molar (M) |
| 6 | $\Delta [cAMP] = \dfrac{V_{max}^{ACIII}}{K_{m}^{ACIII} + [ATP]}$ | Michaelis-Menten kinetics for **Adenylyl Cyclase III** | $V_{max}$ = maximal cyclase velocity; $K_{m}$ ≈ 0.1–0.5 mM ATP | cAMP in µM |
| 7 | $I_{CNG} = g_{CNG} \cdot (V_{m} - E_{rev})$ | **CNG channel current** driving ORN depolarization | $g_{CNG}$ = conductance gated by cAMP; $E_{rev} \approx 0$ mV for Na⁺/Ca²⁺ mix | Current in pA |
| 8 | $[Cl^{-}]_{i}^{ORN} \approx 50$ mM (vs. ~ 4–10 mM in most neurons) | **Special Cl⁻ homeostatic set-point** in ORN cilia (NKCC1-mediated) | NKCC1 = Na-K-2Cl cotransporter | mM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering/Clinical Utility (KTU perspective):**
> - **Anosmia** (loss of smell) is an early biomarker in **Parkinson's disease** and **COVID-19** (SARS-CoV-2 enters via ACE2 on sustentacular cells).
> - **Ageusia** (loss of taste) is a chemotherapy side-effect; targeted TRPM5 antagonists are being developed for type-2 diabetes.
> - **Artificial noses (e-noses)** use metal-oxide or polymer sensor arrays + machine learning to mimic combinatorial olfactory coding.
> - **Sweet-taste receptor (T1R2/T1R3)** modulators (e.g., **sucralose, brazzein**) are used in food-tech and pharma.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS AND ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Beidler Taste Equation from Receptor-Ligand Kinetics

**Starting premise:** A tastant T binds reversibly to a taste receptor R to form a complex TR, with equilibrium constant $K_d$.

$$
R + T \;\xrightleftharpoons[k_{off}]{k_{on}}\; TR
$$

**Step 1 — Define the equilibrium dissociation constant:**

$$
K_{d} \;=\; \dfrac{[R]_{free} \cdot [T]}{[TR]}
$$

Solving for the bound fraction $p = [TR]/[R]_{total}$:

$$
p \;=\; \dfrac{[TR]}{[R]_{total}} \;=\; \dfrac{[TR]}{[R]_{free} + [TR]} \;=\; \dfrac{[T]}{K_{d} + [T]}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume the electrophysiological response R is proportional to the fractional occupancy p:**

$$
R \;=\; R_{max} \cdot p \;=\; R_{max} \cdot \dfrac{[T]}{K_{d} + [T]} \;=\; \dfrac{R_{max} \cdot C}{K_{d} + C}
$$

This is the **Beidler equation**. It is mathematically a rectangular hyperbola (Michaelis-Menten form).

**Step 3 — Limiting cases for examination purposes:**

- At very low concentration ($C \ll K_d$): $R \approx \left(\dfrac{R_{max}}{K_d}\right) C$ → **linear** (sensitive detection regime).
- At very high concentration ($C \gg K_d$): $R \approx R_{max}$ → **saturation**.

**Step 4 — The Hill extension (cooperativity):**

If multiple tastant molecules cooperatively bind the receptor (often relevant for sweeteners with multiple OH groups):

$$
R \;=\; R_{max} \cdot \dfrac{C^{n}}{K_{d}^{\,n} + C^{n}}
$$

with $n$ = **Hill coefficient**. For human sweet receptor (T1R2/T1R3), $n \approx 1.3$–$1.7$ for sugars, indicating modest positive cooperativity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Olfactory CNG Channel Gating by cAMP

**Step 1 — Hill-Langmuir binding of cAMP to CNG channel:**

Each CNG channel has **3 cAMP binding sites** (one per subunit of the homotetramer; for the native heterotetramer CNGA2/CNGB1b, the number is similar).

Fractional activation $P_{open}$:

$$
P_{open} \;=\; \dfrac{[cAMP]^{n}}{K_{d,cAMP}^{\,n} + [cAMP]^{n}}
$$

with $n \approx 1.5$–$2$ for native olfactory CNG.

**Step 2 — Macroscopic current through the open CNG channel:**

$$
I_{CNG} \;=\; N_{channels} \cdot P_{open} \cdot g_{single} \cdot (V_{m} - E_{rev})
$$

where $N_{channels}$ is the number of CNG channels per cilium (~ 100–1000), $g_{single} \approx 10$ pS, $E_{rev} \approx 0$ mV.

**Step 3 — Ca²⁺ entry drives Cl⁻ efflux amplification:**

Each Ca²⁺ entering through the CNG channel binds **calmodulin (CaM)**, which activates **ANO2 (TMEM16B)** Cl⁻ channel:

$$
I_{Cl} \;=\; g_{ANO2} \cdot (V_{m} - E_{Cl})
$$

In ORNs, $E_{Cl} \approx - 26$ mV at body temperature (because $[Cl^-]_i^{ORN} \approx 50$ mM). At resting $V_m \approx - 65$ mV, $E_{Cl} - V_m \approx 39$ mV, so the driving force on Cl⁻ is **outward** (efflux), causing further depolarization.

**Step 4 — Combined transduction gain:**

$$
G_{total} \;=\; \underbrace{1}_{\text{OR activation}} \times \underbrace{\sim 1000}_{\text{Golf amplification}} \times \underbrace{\sim 100}_{\text{CNG gain}} \times \underbrace{\sim 5}_{\text{Cl⁻ amplification}} \;\approx\; 5 \times 10^{5}
$$

This enormous gain explains the **femtomolar sensitivity** of human olfaction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal-to-Noise Optimization in the Glomerular Map

**Step 1 — Define input:** Each glomerulus receives ~ 1000–5000 ORN axons expressing the **same** OR (i.e., tuned to the same ligand).

**Step 2 — Define output:** Each glomerulus drives ~ 20–50 mitral cells.

**Step 3 — Convergence ratio and SNR:**

If each ORN fires with Poisson noise of variance $\sigma^2 = r$ (where r is mean firing rate), then averaging N inputs improves SNR as $\sqrt{N}$:

$$
SNR_{glomerulus} \;=\; \dfrac{r_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}/\sqrt{N}} \;=\; r_{signal} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{\sqrt{r_{baseline}}}
$$

With $N \approx 2500$ axons converging onto a glomerulus, SNR improvement ≈ **50-fold** over a single ORN. This explains why **chemotopic precision** in the bulb is so high despite noisy single-channel events.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Taste Cell Membrane Equation During Salt Taste

For an amiloride-sensitive salt taste response, ENaC channels are the dominant conductance:

**Step 1 — Na⁺ current through ENaC:**

$$
I_{Na} \;=\; g_{ENaC} \cdot (V_{m} - E_{Na})
$$

with $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV at $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM and $[Na^+]_i = 12$ mM.

**Step 2 — GHK application for the receptor potential:**

$$
V_{m}^{salt} \;\approx\; \dfrac{g_{ENaC} \cdot E_{Na} + g_{leak} \cdot E_{leak}}{g_{ENaC} + g_{leak}}
$$

At 100 mM NaCl tastant, $g_{ENaC}$ increases ≈ 3-fold, depolarizing the TRC by ~ 10–15 mV — sufficient to fire action potentials (TRCs are *electrically excitable*, a relatively recent discovery).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Information-Theoretic Capacity of Olfactory Coding

**Step 1 — Receptor repertoire:** $R = 400$ ORs.
**Step 2 — Average number of ORs activated per odorant:** $k \approx 10$–$30$.
**Step 3 — Combinatorial distinct patterns (rough bound):**

$$
N_{patterns} \;\sim\; \binom{R}{k} \;\sim\; 10^{30}\text{–}10^{60}
$$

Even with sparse, correlated, and noisy activation, this combinatorial capacity vastly exceeds the number of distinguishable odorants humans report (~ 10⁴–10⁶), confirming that **combinatorial coding** is sufficient for olfactory discrimination.

---

## 3.6 Summary Matrix — Transduction Mechanisms Across All Five Tastes

| Taste | Receptor | Channel/2nd Messenger | Net Ionic Flux | Afferent Transmitter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sweet | T1R2+T1R3 (GPCR, Class C) | α-gustducin → PLCβ2 → IP₃ → TRPM5 | Na⁺ influx (TRPM5) | ATP via CALHM1/3 |
| Umami | T1R1+T1R3, mGluR1/4 | Same cascade | Na⁺ influx | ATP |
| Bitter | T2R family (Class A/F GPCR) | Same cascade | Na⁺ influx | ATP |
| Salty | ENaC (amiloride-sensitive) | Direct cation channel | Na⁺ influx | ATP + 5-HT |
| Sour | OTOP1 (proton channel); PKD2L1 marker | H⁺ entry, voltage-gated Ca²⁺ influx | H⁺ + Ca²⁺ influx | 5-HT (vesicular, conventional synapse) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1["ODORANT A"] --> C1["RECEPTOR 17 AND 108"]
    O2["ODORANT B"] --> C2["RECEPTOR 17 AND 256"]
    O3["ODORANT C"] --> C3["RECEPTOR 108 AND 256"]
    C1 --> M1["GLOMERULUS 17 ACTIVE"]
    C1 --> M2["GLOMERULUS 108 ACTIVE"]
    C2 --> M1
    C2 --> M3["GLOMERULUS 256 ACTIVE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each odorant activates a **unique combination** of glomeruli (spatial pattern). The piriform cortex decodes this pattern. This is the essence of **combinatorial olfactory coding**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK AND TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q]`
**Outline the molecular events that follow the binding of an odorant to its G-protein-coupled receptor in an olfactory receptor neuron.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(1 mark) Odorant binds an OR (Class A GPCR) on the olfactory cilium; this activates the specialized G-protein **G_olf**, whose α-subunit exchanges GDP for GTP.

(1 mark) Gα_s-GTP stimulates **Adenylyl Cyclase III (ACIII)**, which converts ATP to **cAMP**, raising the local cAMP concentration.

(1 mark) cAMP gates **CNG channels**, allowing Na⁺ and Ca²⁺ influx → depolarization. Influxed Ca²⁺ then opens **ANO2 (TMEM16B) Ca²⁺-activated Cl⁻ channels**, and Cl⁻ efflux amplifies the depolarization, leading to action-potential generation in the ORN axon.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q]`
**State the "one receptor, one neuron" rule for olfactory receptor expression and explain its functional significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(1 mark) Each olfactory receptor neuron (ORN) expresses **only one functional olfactory receptor (OR) gene** out of the ~ 400 functional ORs in humans, in a **monoallelic** manner (only one of the two alleles is expressed).

(1 mark) This is achieved by a stochastic gene-selection mechanism involving a **cis-acting enhancer (the H element)** that activates a single OR promoter and silences all others via a negative-feedback loop.

(1 mark) Functional significance: it ensures that the **response profile of each ORN is molecularly pure** (responsive to a defined set of odorants), which enables **chemotopic mapping** — all ORNs expressing the same OR project to the same glomeruli in the olfactory bulb, preserving odor identity through the wiring.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Q-choice A]`
**(a)** Describe the cellular organization of the olfactory epithelium and the molecular identity of olfactory receptors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the central olfactory pathway from the olfactory bulb to the cerebral cortex and limbic system, highlighting the unique feature that distinguishes it from other sensory modalities. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer to Part (a) — 7 marks:

**Olfactory epithelium (3.5 marks):**
- Located in the upper posterior nasal cavity, covering ~ 2–5 cm² per side, ~ 6 × 10⁶ ORNs in humans.
- Composed of three principal cell types:
  - **Olfactory Receptor Neurons (ORNs):** bipolar neurons; apical dendrite ends in a *knob* bearing **10–20 cilia** that project into the mucus layer; basal unmyelinated axons (bundled as *fila olfactoria*) pass through the **cribriform plate** of the ethmoid bone to synapse in the **olfactory bulb**.
  - **Sustentacular cells:** glia-like supporting cells; express **cytochrome P450** detoxifying enzymes; provide ion/water homeostasis.
  - **Basal (horizontal and globose) cells:** neural stem cells that **continuously regenerate ORNs** throughout adult life (~ 30–60 day turnover); the only constitutive adult neurogenesis in the human nervous system.
- Surrounded by **Bowman's glands** that secrete mucus (traps and solubilizes odorants).

**Olfactory receptors (3.5 marks):**
- Largest gene family in mammals: ~ 1000 OR genes in human, of which only ~ 400 are functional (rest are pseudogenes); rodents have ~ 1100 functional ORs.
- All are **Class A (rhodopsin-like) GPCRs** with seven transmembrane α-helices.
- Each ORN expresses **only one** OR allele (monoallelic, "one receptor-one neuron" rule).
- ORs couple to **G_olf** and require **RTP1/RTP2** chaperones for ciliary membrane localization.
- Individual ORs recognize **multiple odorants** (broad tuning); individual odorants activate **multiple ORs** (combinatorial coding).

#### Model Answer to Part (b) — 7 marks:

**Central olfactory pathway (5 marks):**

$$
\text{ORN} \to \text{Glomerulus} \to \text{Mitral/Tufted cells} \to \text{Piriform cortex (primary)}
$$

- All ORNs expressing the same OR converge onto **1–2 glomeruli** per olfactory bulb (chemotopic map).
- **Mitral and tufted cells** (projection neurons) send axons via the **lateral olfactory tract** to:
  - **Piriform cortex** (3-layered paleocortex; *primary* olfactory cortex) — for odor identification and discrimination.
  - **Anterior olfactory nucleus** — interhemispheric coupling and gain control.
  - **Olfactory tubercle** (ventral striatum) — reward/aversion.
  - **Amygdala** (cortical and medial nuclei) — innate odor valence, fear, mating.
  - **Entorhinal cortex** — olfactory memory.
- Piriform projects to **orbitofrontal cortex (OFC)** via the **mediodorsal thalamus** for conscious odor perception and discrimination.

**Unique feature (2 marks):**
> Olfaction is the **only sensory modality whose first cortical synapse (in piriform cortex) receives input WITHOUT a prior thalamic relay.** Vision, audition, somatosensation, and gustation all have obligatory thalamic relays (LGN, MGN, VPL, VPM). The olfactory→OFC projection is the *only* second-order thalamic relay. This unique anatomy underlies the strong limbic and emotional coupling of smell.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Q-choice B]`
**(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the molecular transduction mechanisms for sweet, umami, and bitter tastes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **labeled-line** versus **combinatorial** coding hypotheses for taste quality coding. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer to Part (a) — 7 marks:

All three qualities (sweet, umami, bitter) use **GPCR-mediated** transduction through a **common downstream cascade**:

**Receptors (3 marks):**
- **Sweet:** T1R2 + T1R3 heterodimer (Class C GPCR); recognizes sugars, artificial sweeteners (saccharin, sucralose), sweet proteins (brazzein, thaumatin), and sweet amino acids.
- **Umami:** T1R1 + T1R3 heterodimer (Class C GPCR); recognizes L-glutamate (free), and is synergistically potentiated by **5'-IMP and 5'-GMP** ribonucleotides. Metabotropic glutamate receptors **mGluR1** and **mGluR4** also contribute in some TRC subtypes.
- **Bitter:** T2R family (~ 25 functional in human, Class A/F GPCRs); broadly tuned — each T2R recognizes many bitter compounds (alkaloids, denatonium, cycloheximide). Individual T2Rs are narrowly tuned, but bitter cells co-express many T2Rs (e.g., a single bitter TRC expresses 4–15 T2Rs).

**Common cascade (4 marks):**

$$
\text{Tastant} + \text{GPCR} \;\to\; \alpha\text{-gustducin (G}\alpha\text{)}\; \to\; \text{PLC}\beta_{2}\; \to\; \text{PIP}_{2} \to \text{IP}_{3} + \text{DAG}
$$

$$
\text{IP}_{3} \to \text{IP}_{3}\text{R}_{3}\; \to\; \uparrow [Ca^{2+}]_{i} \to \text{TRPM5} \to \text{Na}^{+} \text{ influx} \to \text{Depolarization}
$$

- **α-Gustducin** is the taste-specific Gα subunit (analogous to G_olf).
- **PLCβ₂** hydrolyzes PIP₂.
- **IP₃** binds **IP₃R₃** on the endoplasmic reticulum → Ca²⁺ release.
- **TRPM5** (a Ca²⁺-activated monovalent cation channel) → Na⁺ influx → depolarization → ATP release.
- ATP is released via **CALHM1/CALHM3** channels (a recently characterized ATP-permeable channel) → activates **P2X2/P2X3** purinergic receptors on afferent CN VII/IX/X fibers.
- This is **non-vesicular** ATP release — TRCs lack conventional chemical synapses at the apical pore; only Type III cells (sour) form conventional synapses.

#### Model Answer to Part (b) — 7 marks:

**Labeled-line hypothesis (3.5 marks):**
- Each taste quality is encoded by a **dedicated, non-overlapping set of TRCs**, afferent fibers, and central neurons.
- Evidence:
  - **Genetic ablation** of T2R-expressing bitter cells abolishes bitter responses without affecting sweet/umami (genetic knock-out of TRPM5 or PLCβ₂ selectively abolishes Type II cell responses).
  - **Single-fiber recordings:** Each gustatory nerve fiber in the chorda tympani responds best to one quality ("best-stimulus" classification).
  - **Optogenetic activation:** Directly stimulating T2R-expressing bitter cells in mice evokes behavioral aversion even in the absence of any bitter compound (Yarmolinsky et al., 2009-style experiments).
- Type I/II/III cell classification in mouse supports strong labeled-line organization for bitter/sweet/sour.

**Combinatorial/Across-fiber pattern hypothesis (2 marks):**
- Each tastant activates **multiple fiber types** with different response magnitudes; the *pattern* across fibers encodes quality.
- Evidence: Some taste fibers are *broadly* tuned; behavioral responses can be conditioned to **patterns** of activity rather than single-fiber responses.

**Current consensus (1.5 marks):**
- The two models are **not mutually exclusive**.
- **Sweet, umami, bitter, sour** appear to follow labeled-line logic.
- **Salt** may use both amiloride-sensitive (ENaC, labeled) and amiloride-insensitive (combinatorial) pathways.
- The **insular gustatory cortex** uses a more **distributed/spatial code**, with each cortical neuron responding to multiple qualities, integrating peripheral labeled-line information into a unified percept.
- Overall: **peripheral taste = labeled-line; central taste = partially combinatorial/spatial.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose-Marks Traps):**
> 1. **Confusing OR gene count with functional OR count:** The genome has ~ 1000 OR genes, but only ~ 400 are functional in humans; the rest are pseudogenes. Many students write "1000 olfactory receptors" — *incorrect*.
> 2. **Forgetting Cl⁻ amplification:** The unique Ca²⁺-activated Cl⁻ (ANO2) amplification step is a *favorite examiner's question*. Skipping it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong thalamic relay:** Olfaction reaches piriform cortex *without* thalamus. Writing "olfactory information goes through the thalamus to the cortex" loses marks for the part (b) question.
> 4. **Misnaming taste receptors:** Writing "T1R/T2R are ion channels" instead of GPCRs loses the receptor-identification mark. Similarly, calling ENaC a GPCR is incorrect.
> 5. **Skipping ATP/CALHM1:** The non-vesicular ATP release via CALHM1/3 is a *modern* update (post-2013) and frequently tested; older notes still say "vesicular serotonin release from TRCs" — this is true for Type III cells only.
> 6. **Believing the tongue map:** Drawing the classic Hänig tongue map will lose marks. All five qualities are sensed across the entire tongue with modest regional biases.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (read the night before the exam):**

**A. Olfaction (Smell):**
- ☐ **Receptors:** Class A GPCRs, ~ 1000 genes / **~ 400 functional** in human.
- ☐ **One receptor-one neuron rule:** Monoallelic, single OR per ORN.
- ☐ **Transduction cascade:** Odorant → OR → **G_olf** → **ACIII** → **cAMP** → **CNG channel** (Na⁺/Ca²⁺ influx) → **ANO2 (TMEM16B) Cl⁻ channel** → Cl⁻ efflux → depolarization → AP.
- ☐ **Adaptation:** Ca²⁺/CaM reduces CNG sensitivity; **PDE1C/PDE4A** hydrolyze cAMP.
- ☐ **ORN lifespan:** ~ 30–60 days; **continuous neurogenesis** from basal cells.
- ☐ **Convergence:** All ORNs with the same OR → **1–2 glomeruli** in olfactory bulb (chemotopic map).
- ☐ **Central pathway:** Glomerulus → mitral/tufted → **piriform cortex** (no thalamic relay at first synapse!) → OFC (via MD thalamus), amygdala, entorhinal cortex, hippocampus.
- ☐ **Coding:** **Combinatorial** — odor identity = unique spatial/temporal pattern of glomerular activation.
- ☐ **Sensitivity:** Threshold ~ 10⁻¹² M; transduction gain ~ 5 × 10⁵.

**B. Gustation (Taste):**
- ☐ **Receptor cells:** TRCs in taste buds, ~ 50–100 per bud; **turnover ~ 10–14 days**.
- ☐ **Innervation:** CN VII (anterior 2/3), CN IX (posterior 1/3), CN X (epiglottis).
- ☐ **Five qualities:** Sweet (T1R2/T1R3), Umami (T1R1/T1R3, mGluR1/4), Bitter (**T2R family ~25**), Salty (**ENaC**), Sour (**OTOP1** proton channel).
- ☐ **Type II cell cascade (sweet/umami/bitter):** GPCR → α-gustducin → PLCβ2 → IP₃ → TRPM5 → depolarization → **ATP release via CALHM1/3** → **P2X2/3** on afferents.
- ☐ **Type III cells (sour):** Use **OTOP1** + conventional **5-HT** vesicular release.
- ☐ **Central pathway:** TRC → CN VII/IX/X → **rNST (rostral NST)** → **VPMpc thalamus** → **insular/frontal opercular cortex** → OFC.
- ☐ **rNST also projects to:** amygdala (valence), hypothalamus (cephalic-phase reflexes), brainstem reticular formation.
- ☐ **Coding:** Mostly **labeled-line** peripherally; somewhat combinatorial centrally.
- ☐ **Myth to avoid:** The classic "tongue map" is wrong; all qualities are sensed across the entire tongue.

**C. Key Comparisons (favourite comparative essay questions):**
- ☐ Olfaction vs. Gustation: receptor count (~ 400 vs. ~ 30), coding (combinatorial vs. labeled-line), thalamic relay (no vs. yes), modality count (thousands vs. 5).
- ☐ CNG vs. ENaC vs. TRPM5: ionotropic vs. ionotropic vs. Ca²⁺-gated.
- ☐ α-gustducin vs. G_olf: taste-specific vs. olfactory-specific Gα.

**D. Clinical Correlations (essay-essay favorite):**
- ☐ **Anosmia** — early sign of **Parkinson's disease**, **COVID-19** (ACE2 on sustentacular cells).
- ☐ **Ageusia** — chemotherapy-induced; zinc deficiency.
- ☐ **Specific anosmia** — genetic inability to detect one compound (e.g., androstenone, isovaleric acid).
- ☐ **Phenylthiocarbamide (PTC) tasting** — Mendelian trait, TAS2R38 polymorphism.

**E. Engineering/Biotech Applications:**
- ☐ **Electronic noses (e-noses):** Mimic combinatorial coding via sensor arrays + ML.
- ☐ **Sweet-taste receptor modulators:** Sucralose, brazzein in food-tech.
- ☐ **Bitter-masking compounds:** Block T2R for pediatric drug formulations.
- ☐ **TRPM5 antagonists:** Under study for type-2 diabetes (modulate postprandial insulin release).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Synaptic Transmission** is the fundamental neurobiological process by which an electrical or chemical signal is propagated from one neuron (the *presynaptic neuron*) to another neuron, muscle cell, or gland cell (the *postsynaptic cell*) across a specialized intercellular junction known as a **synapse**. It is the principal mechanism underlying all information processing in the central nervous system (CNS) and peripheral nervous system (PNS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synapse** (term coined by Sir Charles Scott Sherrington, 1897): A specialized junction that mediates the transfer of information between two excitable cells. The **synaptic cleft** is the narrow gap (~20–40 nm) separating the presynaptic and postsynaptic membranes.

There are two principal morphological classes of synapses:
- **Electrical Synapse** – Direct ionic/electrical coupling via **gap junctions** (connexons).
- **Chemical Synapse** – Signal propagation mediated by **neurotransmitter (NT)** molecules released from presynaptic vesicles.

> [!NOTE]
> In the mammalian brain, **chemical synapses** vastly outnumber electrical synapses. The KTU 2024 Neurobiology syllabus places strong emphasis on chemical transmission.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Postal Service" of the Brain

Imagine two adjacent apartments separated by a thin wall (the synaptic cleft). The resident of Apartment A (presynaptic neuron) cannot simply shout across to Apartment B (postsynaptic neuron) because the wall is too thick. Instead:

1. Apartment A prepares a **letter** (neurotransmitter packet/vesicle).
2. The letter is **handed off** through a **mail slot** in the wall (voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels) when an authorized "doorbell" rings (action potential arrives).
3. A specialized **courier** (vesicle fusion machinery – SNARE proteins) carries the letter across.
4. Apartment B's resident reads the letter using a **special key** (postsynaptic receptor) and acts on it (opens ion channels, triggers second messenger cascades).

If the doorbell never rings, the letter never gets sent — this is the **all-or-none gating principle** of synaptic transmission.

---

### 1.3 Standard Quantitative Metrics in Synaptic Transmission

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key parameters (memorize for KTU exams):**
> - **Synaptic cleft width (chemical synapse):** **20–40 nm**
> - **Synaptic delay (chemical synapse):** **0.5–2 ms**
> - **Synaptic delay (electrical synapse):** **~0.1 ms** (virtually instantaneous)
> - **Quantal size (miniature EPSP):** **~0.1–1 mV**
> - **Resting membrane potential:** **−70 mV**
> - **Threshold for action potential:** **~−55 mV**

---

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Chemical Synaptic Transmission (Step-by-Step)

A chemical synapse functions through a tightly orchestrated 6-stage sequence. Each step is a potential target for pharmaceutical modulation (and therefore a frequent KTU exam topic).

**Step 1 — Synthesis of Neurotransmitter**
- Small-molecule transmitters (glutamate, GABA, acetylcholine, dopamine) are synthesized in the **presynaptic terminal** by specific enzymes.
- Neuropeptides are synthesized in the **cell body (soma)** and transported axonally.

**Step 2 — Vesicular Loading (Packaging)**
- Neurotransmitters are loaded into **synaptic vesicles** (diameter ~40–50 nm) via specific transporters:
  - **VGLUT** (Vesicular Glutamate Transporter) for glutamate.
  - **VAChT** (Vesicular Acetylcholine Transporter) for ACh.
  - **VMAT** (Vesicular Monoamine Transporter) for monoamines.
- The **proton gradient** (vesicular H⁺-ATPase generates ΔμH⁺) powers vesicular loading.

**Step 3 — Action Potential Invasion & Ca²⁺ Entry**
- The action potential (AP) reaches the **axon terminal**, depolarizing it.
- **Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels** (P/Q-type and N-type) open in response to depolarization.
- Extracellular [Ca²⁺] (~2 mM) >> intracellular [Ca²⁺] (~100 nM), producing a steep electrochemical driving force.

**Step 4 — Vesicle Docking, Priming, and Fusion (Exocytosis)**
- Ca²⁺ binds to **synaptotagmin** (the Ca²⁺ sensor).
- **SNARE complex** assembly (syntaxin + SNAP-25 on membrane, synaptobrevin/VAMP on vesicle) drives membrane fusion.
- NTs are released into the **synaptic cleft** via exocytosis.

**Step 5 — Postsynaptic Receptor Activation**
- NTs diffuse across the cleft (~20–40 nm; diffusion time < 1 ms).
- Bind to two principal receptor classes:
  - **Ionotropic receptors** (ligand-gated ion channels) — fast (sub-millisecond).
  - **Metabotropic receptors** (GPCRs) — slow (100s of ms to seconds).

**Step 6 — Termination of Signal**
- Three principal mechanisms:
  1. **Enzymatic degradation** (e.g., acetylcholinesterase for ACh).
  2. **Reuptake** by transporters (e.g., DAT, SERT, NET, EAATs).
  3. **Diffusion** away from the cleft.

---

### 2.2 Electrical vs Chemical Synapses — Comparative Analysis

| Feature | Electrical Synapse | Chemical Synapse |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupling mechanism** | Gap junctions (connexons) | Neurotransmitter release |
| **Cleft width** | ~3.5 nm | 20–40 nm |
| **Synaptic delay** | < 0.1 ms | 0.5–2 ms |
| **Directionality** | Mostly bidirectional | Unidirectional |
| **Signal fidelity** | High (1:1 AP coupling) | Variable (can amplify or filter) |
| **Plasticity** | Limited | High (LTP/LTD possible) |
| **Examples** | Retinal amacrine cells, hippocampal interneurons | Most CNS/PNS synapses |
| **Connexin proteins** | Connexin-36 (neuronal) | N/A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Electrical synapses are preferred in neural circuits requiring ultra-fast, synchronous activity (e.g., escape reflexes in invertebrates). Chemical synapses dominate in circuits requiring **computational complexity, gain control, and plasticity**.

---

### 2.3 Postsynaptic Potentials — The Computational Substrate

**Excitatory Postsynaptic Potential (EPSP):**
- Depolarization of the postsynaptic membrane.
- Typically mediated by **Na⁺ influx** (through AMPA or NMDA glutamate receptors).
- Reversal potential $E_{\text{EPSP}} \approx 0$ mV.

**Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potential (IPSP):**
- Hyperpolarization (or shunting inhibition).
- Typically mediated by **Cl⁻ influx** (GABA$_A$ receptors) or **K⁺ efflux** (GABA$_B$ receptors).
- Reversal potential $E_{\text{IPSP}} \approx -70$ mV for Cl⁻.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Definition | Equation / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_{\text{ion}}$ | Nernst (reversal) potential | $E_{\text{ion}} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[ion]_{\text{out}}}{[ion]_{\text{in}}}$ | mV |
| $V_m$ | Membrane potential | Weighted sum of $E_{\text{ion}}$ | mV |
| $E_{\text{Na}}$ | Sodium equilibrium potential | **+60 mV** | mV |
| $E_{\text{K}}$ | Potassium equilibrium potential | **−90 mV** (typical) | mV |
| $E_{\text{Cl}}$ | Chloride equilibrium potential | **−70 mV** (in mature neurons) | mV |
| $E_{\text{Ca}}$ | Calcium equilibrium potential | **+120 to +140 mV** | mV |
| $I_{\text{PSC}}$ | Postsynaptic current | $I = g_{\text{syn}} (V_m - E_{\text{ion}})$ | A |
| $g_{\text{syn}}$ | Synaptic conductance | Time-dependent variable | S (Siemens) |
| $\tau_m$ | Membrane time constant | $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ | seconds |
| $Q_m$ | Quantal content (release) | $Q_m = N \cdot p$ (Bin's formula) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bin's Formula (Quantal Analysis):** $Q_m = N \cdot p$, where $N$ = number of release sites, $p$ = probability of release at each site. **MEPP** (Miniature End-Plate Potential) amplitude represents the response to a single vesicle ($Q = 1$).

**Constants to memorize:**
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $\dfrac{RT}{F}$ at 37 °C ≈ **26.7 mV**

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Medical Utility

- **Pharmacology:** Every major psychiatric/neurological drug (SSRIs, antipsychotics, anesthetics) targets a specific stage of synaptic transmission.
- **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Understanding synaptic plasticity is essential for designing adaptive neural prosthetics.
- **Computational Neuroscience:** Synaptic transmission forms the substrate of **artificial neural networks (ANNs)**, where each "weight" in an ANN is conceptually analogous to synaptic strength $g_{\text{syn}}$.
- **Toxicology:** Neurotoxins (e.g., **tetrodotoxin** blocks Na⁺ channels; **ω-conotoxin** blocks N-type Ca²⁺ channels; **botulinum toxin** cleaves SNAP-25) disrupt specific steps.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Nernst Equation (Reversal Potential of an Ion)

The Nernst equation is the foundation for understanding ion flow during synaptic transmission. It gives the membrane potential at which there is **no net ionic current** for a given ion.

**Starting point — electrochemical equilibrium:**

At equilibrium, the chemical force (concentration gradient) and the electrical force (membrane potential) acting on the ion are equal and opposite. The work required to move one mole of ions from outside to inside the cell is:

$$W_{\text{electrical}} = z F (V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}})$$

The work done by the concentration gradient (chemical potential difference) is:

$$W_{\text{chemical}} = RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{\text{in}}}{[ion]_{\text{out}}}$$

**Step 1 — Setting chemical and electrical work equal:**

$$zF(V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}) = RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{\text{in}}}{[ion]_{\text{out}}}$$

**Step 2 — Defining $E_{\text{ion}} = V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}$:**

$$zF E_{\text{ion}} = RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{\text{in}}}{[ion]_{\text{out}}}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $E_{\text{ion}}$:**

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{\text{in}}}{[ion]_{\text{out}}}$$

**Step 4 — Converting to log base 10 and inserting physiological temperature (37 °C ≈ 310 K):**

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{\text{in}}}{[ion]_{\text{out}}}$$

$$\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}\bigg\vert_{37\,°C} \approx 61.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Final Nernst equation used in KTU examinations:**

$$E_{\text{ion}} \approx \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_{\text{in}}}{[ion]_{\text{out}}} \text{ mV}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Given:
- $[K^+]_{\text{in}} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{\text{out}} = 5$ mM, $z = +1$.

$$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{140}{5}\right) = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(28) = 61.5 \times 1.447$$

$$E_K \approx 89 \text{ mV} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_m \text{ tends toward } -89 \text{ mV}$$

(Note: Sign convention — for cations, $E_{\text{ion}}$ is positive inward; the equilibrium membrane potential is written as $-E_K$ for extracellular-positive reference, hence the typical literature value of $E_K = -89$ mV.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

When multiple ions contribute to the resting membrane potential, the **GHK equation** provides a weighted average using permeabilities $P$.

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_{K}[K^+]_{\text{out}} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{\text{out}} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{\text{in}}}{P_{K}[K^+]_{\text{in}} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{\text{in}} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{\text{out}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At rest, $P_K \gg P_{Na} \gg P_{Cl}$. Therefore, $V_m \approx E_K$. This is why opening K⁺ channels hyperpolarizes the cell.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quantal Release (Bin's Formulation)

The **quantal hypothesis** (Katz and Miledi, Nobel Prize 1970) states that NT is released in discrete packets (quanta), each representing the contents of one synaptic vesicle.

**Bin's empirical equation:**

$$Q_m = N \cdot p$$

- $Q_m$ = mean quantal content of the EPSP.
- $N$ = number of release sites (docked vesicles).
- $p$ = average probability of release per site.

**Coefficient of variation test for $N$ and $p$:**

$$\frac{\text{Var}(m)}{\text{mean}(m)} = \frac{1}{N} \left(1 - p\right)$$

If $p$ is high ($\to 1$), variance/mean → 0; if $p$ is small, variance/mean → $1/N$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Synaptic Current & Conductance

The current flowing through a postsynaptic receptor-channel complex is:

$$I_{\text{PSC}} = g_{\text{syn}}(t) \cdot \left(V_m(t) - E_{\text{ion}}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Assume an instantaneous rise and exponential decay of $g_{\text{syn}}$:**

$$g_{\text{syn}}(t) = \bar{g} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{\text{syn}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$I_{\text{PSC}}(t) = \bar{g} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{\text{syn}}} \cdot \left(V_m(t) - E_{\text{ion}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — For small $V_m$ perturbations near rest, approximate $V_m(t) \approx V_{\text{rest}}$:**

$$I_{\text{PSC}}(t) \approx \bar{g} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{\text{syn}}} \cdot \left(V_{\text{rest}} - E_{\text{ion}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Postsynaptic potential (via cable equation for an isopotential patch):**

$$\Delta V(t) = \frac{I_{\text{PSC}}(t)}{C_m} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

For brief synaptic currents where $t \ll \tau_m$:

$$\Delta V_{\text{peak}} \approx \frac{Q_{\text{PSC}}}{C_m} \quad\text{where}\quad Q_{\text{PSC}} = \int I_{\text{PSC}}(t)\, dt$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ionotropic vs Metabotropic Receptors

| Feature | Ionotropic Receptors | Metabotropic Receptors |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Multi-subunit ligand-gated ion channel | Single GPCR (7-TM) |
| **Speed** | Sub-millisecond | 100s of ms – seconds |
| **Mechanism** | Direct ion flow | G-protein → second messenger |
| **Examples** | AMPA, NMDA, GABA$_A$, nAChR | mGluR, GABA$_B$, D1/D2, β-adrenergic |
| **Effect on $V_m$** | Fast EPSP/IPSP | Slow modulation, plasticity |
| **Ion flow** | Direct | Indirect (e.g., via K⁺ channel closure) |
| **Plasticity role** | NMDA = coincidence detector | mGluR = modulatory |

---

### 3.6 Synaptic Integration — The "Decision-Making" of a Neuron

A single EPSP is rarely sufficient to fire an AP. Postsynaptic neurons **integrate** thousands of inputs.

**Two principal forms of integration:**

1. **Spatial summation:**
   $$V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{EPSP}_i$$
   (Simultaneous inputs from different synapses add linearly on the dendrite/soma.)

2. **Temporal summation:**
   $$V_{\text{total}}(t) = \sum_{k} \Delta V(t - t_k) \cdot e^{-(t-t_k)/\tau_m}$$
   (Sequential inputs at the same synapse add, weighted by the membrane time constant.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dendritic computation:** Real dendrites are not passive cables — they contain voltage-gated channels (e.g., back-propagating APs) and NMDA receptors that act as **coincidence detectors** (see Bienenstock-Cooper-Munro rule in synaptic plasticity).

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Chemical Synapse — Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[AP arrives at axon terminal] --> A2{Depolarization of presynaptic terminal}
    A2 --> A3[Voltage-gated Ca2+ channels OPEN]
    A3 --> A4[Ca2+ influx into presynaptic terminal]
    A4 --> A5[Ca2+ binds synaptotagmin on vesicle]
    A5 --> A6[SNARE complex assembles]
    A6 --> A7[Vesicle fuses with presynaptic membrane]
    A7 --> A8[Neurotransmitter released into synaptic cleft]
    A8 --> A9{NT diffuses across cleft 20-40 nm}
    A9 --> A10[Binds ionotropic receptor FAST EPSP IPSP]
    A9 --> A11[Binds metabotropic receptor SLOW modulation]
    A10 --> A12[Postsynaptic depolarization or hyperpolarization]
    A11 --> A13[G protein cascade and second messenger]
    A12 --> A14{Summation threshold reached?}
    A14 --> A15[YES Action Potential fired]
    A14 --> A16[NO subthreshold response decays]
    A13 --> A17[Modulation of excitability or gene expression]
    A8 --> A18[Termination: Reuptake / Enzymatic degradation / Diffusion]
```

---

### 4.2 Synaptic Vesicle Cycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Reserve pool vesicles] --> B2[Recruited to active zone]
    B2 --> B3[Docked at membrane]
    B3 --> B4[Primed ready state]
    B4 --> B5[Ca2+ trigger]
    B5 --> B6[Fusion and exocytosis]
    B6 --> B7[Endocytosis clathrin mediated]
    B7 --> B8[Endosomal sorting]
    B8 --> B9[New vesicles recycled]
    B9 --> B2
    B6 --> B10[Full collapse fusion]
    B10 --> B11[Kiss and run]
    B11 --> B2
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Synaptic Integration Leading to Action Potential

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Multiple presynaptic inputs] --> C2{Inputs at excitatory synapses}
    C2 --> C3[Each generates EPSP]
    C3 --> C4[Spatial summation at axon hillock]
    C2 --> C5{Inputs at inhibitory synapses}
    C5 --> C6[Each generates IPSP]
    C6 --> C7[Subtract from excitation]
    C7 --> C4
    C3 --> C8[Temporal summation over time constant tau m]
    C8 --> C4
    C4 --> C9{Net Vm at axon hillock crosses -55 mV}
    C9 --> C10[YES AP fires]
    C9 --> C11[NO no AP signal decays]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Comparison of Electrical vs Chemical Synapse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Presynaptic neuron] -->|Gap junction connexons bidirectional| D2[Postsynaptic neuron]
    D3[Presynaptic terminal] -->|AP arrival Ca2+ entry| D4[NT release into cleft]
    D4 -->|Diffusion 20-40 nm| D5[Receptor binding]
    D5 -->|Ion flow or signaling cascade| D6[Postsynaptic response]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "synapse." Differentiate between electrical and chemical synapses with any three distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **synapse** is a specialized intercellular junction that permits the transfer of information from one excitable cell to another (1 mark).

Three distinguishing features (½ mark each, total 1½ marks):

| Electrical Synapse | Chemical Synapse |
|---|---|
| Gap junction (connexons) coupling | Neurotransmitter-mediated |
| Cleft ~3.5 nm | Cleft 20–40 nm |
| Synaptic delay < 0.1 ms | Synaptic delay 0.5–2 ms |
| Mostly bidirectional | Unidirectional |
| No amplification | Signal amplification possible |

Closing summary statement (½ mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the role of calcium ions in neurotransmitter release at a chemical synapse.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. When the action potential reaches the presynaptic terminal, voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels open (1 mark).
2. Ca²⁺ flows into the terminal down its steep electrochemical gradient (extra [Ca²⁺]ₒ ~2 mM vs [Ca²⁺]ᵢ ~100 nM) (1 mark).
3. Ca²⁺ binds to **synaptotagmin** on synaptic vesicles, triggering the SNARE-mediated fusion of vesicles with the presynaptic membrane and the release of neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q3. (a) Describe the sequential events that occur during chemical synaptic transmission. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Synthesis & storage (1 mark):** NTs are synthesized in the presynaptic terminal (small-molecule NTs) or soma (peptides) and loaded into synaptic vesicles via vesicular transporters (VGLUT, VAChT, VMAT).
2. **Action potential arrival & Ca²⁺ entry (1 mark):** Depolarization opens voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels (N- and P/Q-type); Ca²⁺ flows in.
3. **Vesicle fusion (exocytosis) (1 mark):** Ca²⁺-synaptotagmin binding triggers SNARE complex assembly; vesicle fuses with presynaptic membrane; NTs released into the cleft.
4. **Diffusion & receptor binding (1 mark):** NTs diffuse across the 20–40 nm cleft; bind postsynaptic receptors (ionotropic → fast EPSP/IPSP; metabotropic → slow modulation).
5. **Postsynaptic response (1 mark):** Ion flow changes $V_m$ or activates G-protein cascades; produces EPSP/IPSP.
6. **Termination (1 mark):** NT cleared by reuptake (transporters), enzymatic degradation (AChE, MAO), or diffusion.
7. **Conclusion (1 mark):** All steps are tightly regulated and represent pharmacological targets.

---

**(b) Explain the mechanism of excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials. Add a note on synaptic integration. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**EPSP (3 marks):**
- Mediated by **excitatory NTs** (glutamate, ACh at muscle) acting on ionotropic receptors.
- **AMPA receptors:** Permit Na⁺ influx → depolarization. Reversal potential $E_{\text{AMPA}} \approx 0$ mV.
- **NMDA receptors:** Permeable to Na⁺ and Ca²⁺; blocked by Mg²⁺ at resting $V_m$; require depolarization + glutamate binding (coincidence detection) → Ca²⁺ entry triggers LTP.

**IPSP (2 marks):**
- Mediated by **inhibitory NTs** (GABA, glycine).
- **GABA$_A$:** Cl⁻ influx → hyperpolarization. $E_{\text{Cl}} \approx -70$ mV in mature neurons.
- **GABA$_B$:** Metabotropic; opens K⁺ channels via G$_i$ → K⁺ efflux → hyperpolarization.

**Synaptic Integration (2 marks):**
- **Spatial summation:** Multiple simultaneous EPSPs/IPSPs at different sites add algebraically at the axon hillock.
- **Temporal summation:** Sequential EPSPs at the same site add within the membrane time constant $\tau_m$.
- If net depolarization at the axon hillock reaches the threshold (≈ −55 mV), an AP is fired.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**

**Q4. (a) With a neat diagram, describe the structure of a chemical synapse. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Diagram description (3 marks):** Refer to a labeled figure of the chemical synapse showing:
- **Presynaptic terminal** with mitochondria, synaptic vesicles, and active zone.
- **Synaptic cleft** (20–40 nm).
- **Postsynaptic membrane** with receptors and postsynaptic density (PSD).
- **Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels** on the presynaptic side.
- **Neurotransmitter molecules** in the cleft.

**Structural description (4 marks):**
1. **Presynaptic terminal:** Contains synaptic vesicles (~40 nm diameter), mitochondria (for ATP), and the active zone with docked vesicles. (1 mark)
2. **Synaptic cleft:** A 20–40 nm wide extracellular space containing extracellular matrix and cell-adhesion molecules (e.g., neurexins, neuroligins). (1 mark)
3. **Postsynaptic membrane:** Specialized region with densely packed receptors and the **postsynaptic density (PSD)** — a protein scaffold containing PSD-95, Homer, GKAP, Shank. (1 mark)
4. **Receptor types:** Ionotropic (fast) and metabotropic (slow) receptors embedded in the postsynaptic membrane. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Compare electrical and chemical synapses. Discuss the significance of synaptic plasticity in learning and memory. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Electrical | Chemical |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Gap junctions | Neurotransmitter release |
| Cleft | ~3.5 nm | 20–40 nm |
| Delay | < 0.1 ms | 0.5–2 ms |
| Direction | Often bidirectional | Unidirectional |
| Plasticity | Limited | High (LTP/LTD) |
| Fidelity | 1:1 | Variable / amplifiable |
| Function | Synchronization | Computation, learning |

**Synaptic plasticity in learning & memory (3 marks):**
1. **Long-Term Potentiation (LTP):** Persistent strengthening of synapses based on recent patterns of activity. In CA1 hippocampus: high-frequency stimulation → glutamate release → NMDA receptor activation → Ca²⁺ entry → CaMKII activation → AMPAR insertion → enhanced EPSP. (1.5 marks)
2. **Long-Term Depression (LTD):** Persistent weakening of synaptic strength via low-frequency stimulation → modest Ca²⁺ → phosphatase activation → AMPAR removal. (1 mark)
3. **Significance:** Hebbian plasticity ("neurons that fire together, wire together") is the cellular basis of learning and memory; defects are implicated in Alzheimer's disease, intellectual disability, and addiction. (0.5 mark)
