topics.title: Electrical Properties of Neuron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT752 - NEUROBIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Properties of Neuron

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **electrical properties of a neuron** refer to the bioelectrochemical phenomena that govern how nerve cells generate, propagate, and transmit electrical signals across their plasma membrane. These properties are dictated by the unequal distribution of ions (primarily **Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, and Ca²⁺**) across the lipid bilayer, the selective permeability of ion channels, and the activity of electrogenic pumps (notably the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase**).

Three foundational electrical concepts define neuronal excitability:

1. **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP):** The steady-state voltage difference (typically **−70 mV** inside relative to outside) maintained when the neuron is not firing.
2. **Action Potential (AP):** A rapid, all-or-none depolarization-repolarization wave (peaking near **+30 mV to +40 mV**) that propagates along the axon to convey information.
3. **Cable Properties:** The passive electrotonic spread of sub-threshold voltages governed by membrane resistance, axial resistance, and membrane capacitance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every KTU exam question on this topic tests the **mechanism of ion flow**, the **mathematical basis of equilibrium potentials (Nernst & GHK equations)**, and the **biophysical phases of the action potential** (depolarization, repolarization, hyperpolarization, refractory periods).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery in a Saltwater Tank"

Imagine a neuron as a **leaky battery submerged in a saltwater tank**:

- The **lipid bilayer** is the battery's insulating shell.
- **Ion channels** are the selectively open valves on the battery's terminals — some only let Na⁺ through (like a one-way valve), others only K⁺.
- The **Na⁺/K⁺ pump** is a tireless worker who constantly hauls 3 Na⁺ ions *out* and 2 K⁺ ions *in* per ATP cycle, building up the stored charge.
- When you press a "trigger" (a stimulus reaching threshold, **−55 mV**), the Na⁺ valves burst open — this is **depolarization**.
- Then K⁺ valves open, pushing charge back out — this is **repolarization**.
- The signal travels down the "wire" (axon) just like current flowing down a copper cable, but because the axon is insulated by myelin, the signal **jumps** between nodes (saltatory conduction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why neurons use electricity:** Evolution selected electrochemical signaling because it is **fast (≤ 120 m/s)**, **long-range**, and **regenerative** — each stretch of membrane can re-amplify the signal, unlike passive diffusion.

---

### 1.3 The Ionic Basis — Key Players

| Ion | Intracellular (mM) | Extracellular (mM) | Equilibrium Potential (E, mV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| K⁺ | ~140 | ~5 | **−90** |
| Na⁺ | ~15 | ~145 | **+60** |
| Cl⁻ | ~10 | ~110 | **−70** |
| Ca²⁺ | ~0.0001 | ~1.2 | **+120** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam shortcut:** Remember "**K inside, Na outside**" — the concentration gradients drive the action potential. The inside is **negative at rest** because the membrane is far more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺ at rest.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Resting Membrane Potential — Step-by-Step Logic

The RMP arises because:

1. **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** actively extrudes 3 Na⁺ out / imports 2 K⁺ in per cycle → net loss of 1 positive charge from inside.
2. At rest, the membrane is **~25× more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺** (due to leak channels).
3. K⁺ flows *down* its concentration gradient outward, leaving behind non-diffusible anions (proteins, phosphates) → inside becomes negative.
4. Equilibrium is reached when the **electrical gradient pulling K⁺ in** balances the **chemical gradient pushing K⁺ out**.

This balance point for any single ion is its **Nernst (Equilibrium) Potential**.

---

### 2.2 The Action Potential — Five Sequential Phases

| Phase | Voltage Range | Dominant Event | Channel Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resting** | −70 mV | Leak currents balance | K⁺ leak channels open; Na⁺ closed |
| **Depolarization** | −70 → +30 mV | Rapid Na⁺ influx | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels OPEN |
| **Repolarization** | +30 → −70 mV | K⁺ efflux | Na⁺ channels INACTIVATE; K⁺ channels OPEN |
| **Hyperpolarization** | −70 → −80 mV | Excess K⁺ efflux | K⁺ channels slow to close (delayed rectifier) |
| **Return to RMP** | −80 → −70 mV | Na⁺/K⁺ pump restores gradients | Pump actively restores ion distribution |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — every KTU numerical and derivation question is built from them.**

| Formula | Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Nernst Equation | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | Equilibrium potential of a single ion |
| Nernst (log₁₀ form, 37 °C) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | Simplified version used in labs |
| GHK Voltage Equation | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$ | Membrane potential with multiple permeant ions |
| Membrane Time Constant | $\tau = R_m \cdot C_m$ | How fast voltage changes with current |
| Length Constant | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | Distance a passive signal decays by 1/e |
| Cable Equation | $\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2} V$ | Steady-state passive voltage spread |
| Threshold | $V_{th} \approx -55 \text{ mV}$ | Minimum depolarization to fire AP |
| Conductance Ratio (rest) | $g_K : g_{Na} : g_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ | Relative permeabilities at rest |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to memorize for KTU:**
> - R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - F = **96,485 C/mol** (Faraday's constant)
> - T = **310 K** (at body temperature, 37 °C)
> - RT/F at 37 °C ≈ **26.7 mV**

---

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering & Medicine

- **Neural prosthetics & brain-computer interfaces (BCIs):** Engineers model neurons using **Hodgkin-Huxley differential equations** to design cochlear implants, retinal prostheses, and deep brain stimulators.
- **EEG/ECoG signal processing:** The summed ionic currents from millions of neurons form measurable scalp potentials — biomedical engineers must understand cable properties to interpret these signals.
- **Drug discovery:** Most neuropharmacological agents (lidocaine, tetrodotoxin, phenytoin) act by **blocking voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** — knowledge of the action potential is essential.
- **AI hardware inspiration:** Memristors and neuromorphic chips mimic the **leak-integrate-fire** dynamics of neurons.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation

Consider a single permeant ion $X^{z+}$ at concentrations $[X]_{in}$ and $[X]_{out}$ across a membrane. At equilibrium, the **electrochemical potential** is equal on both sides.

**Step 1:** Write the electrochemical potential difference:

$$\Delta \mu = RT \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} + z F (V_{in} - V_{out})$$

**Step 2:** At equilibrium, $\Delta \mu = 0$ (no net driving force). The Nernst potential $E_X$ is defined such that $V_{in} - V_{out} = E_X$.

$$0 = RT \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} + z F E_X$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for $E_X$:

$$E_X = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

**Step 4:** By inverting the log argument using $-\ln(a/b) = \ln(b/a)$, the conventional form becomes:

$$\boxed{E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to log₁₀ using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E_X = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

**Step 6:** At 37 °C, $\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \approx 61 \text{ mV}$. So:

$$E_X \approx \frac{61}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} \quad \text{(mV)}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Equilibrium Potential of K⁺

Given: $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM, $z = +1$, T = 37 °C.

**Step 1:** Apply the simplified Nernst equation:

$$E_K = \frac{61}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{5}{140}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{5}{140} = 0.03571$$

**Step 3:** Take log₁₀:

$$\log_{10}(0.03571) = -1.447$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by 61:

$$E_K = 61 \times (-1.447) = -88.3 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -88 \text{ mV} \;\; (\text{matches KTU standard table value} \approx -90 \text{ mV})}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Cable Equation (1-D Passive Spread)

For a passive axon modeled as a cylinder of radius $a$, with intracellular axial resistance per unit length $r_i$ (Ω/cm) and membrane resistance per unit length $r_m$ (Ω·cm):

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's current law at a node: axial current in = axial current out + membrane leak.

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = \frac{r_i}{r_m} V$$

**Step 2:** Define the length constant $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute to obtain the canonical cable equation:

$$\boxed{\lambda^2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = V \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = \frac{V}{\lambda^2}}$$

**Step 4:** Steady-state solution for an injected current at $x=0$:

$$V(x) = V_0 \, e^{-x/\lambda}$$

This shows the voltage decays **exponentially** with distance — and **myelination increases $\lambda$**, allowing faster, farther passive spread.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Problem — Action Potential Threshold

A neuron's membrane has $C_m = 1 \, \mu F/cm^2$ and $R_m = 2000 \, \Omega \cdot cm^2$. A synaptic current of $I = 5 \text{ nA}$ is injected.

**Step 1:** Compute the time constant:

$$\tau = R_m \cdot C_m = 2000 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2:** Voltage change for a brief pulse:

$$\Delta V = \frac{I \cdot \Delta t}{C_m}$$

For $\Delta t = 0.5$ ms:

$$\Delta V = \frac{5 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3:** Since threshold requires a rise from −70 mV to −55 mV (ΔV = 15 mV), a single pulse is insufficient — **temporal summation** of multiple synaptic inputs is needed.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Nernst vs. GHK vs. Threshold

| Feature | Nernst | GHK | Threshold Concept |
|---|---|---|---|
| Applies to | Single ion | Multiple ions simultaneously | Trigger for AP |
| Permeability used | Implicit (=1) | Weighted by permeability P | N/A |
| Used clinically | Ion channel reversal potentials | Realistic RMP prediction | All-or-none firing rule |
| KTU mark weight | 5 marks (derivation) | 7 marks (full question) | 3 marks (definition) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Action Potential Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resting Potential -70 mV] --> B{Stimulus reaches threshold -55 mV?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Voltage-gated Na+ channels OPEN]
    C --> D[Rapid depolarization to +30 mV]
    D --> E[Na+ channels INACTIVATE]
    E --> F[Voltage-gated K+ channels OPEN]
    F --> G[Repolarization back to -70 mV]
    G --> H[K+ channels slow to close]
    H --> I[Hyperpolarization -80 mV]
    I --> J[Na+/K+ pump restores gradients]
    J --> A
    H --> K[Absolute Refractory Period]
    G --> L[Relative Refractory Period]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hodgkin-Huxley Circuit Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Membrane_Model[Hodgkin Huxley Equivalent Circuit]
        Cm[Membrane Capacitance Cm]
        Rleak[Leak Conductance gleak]
        Gna[Voltage-gated Na+ Conductance gNa]
        Gk[Voltage-gated K+ Conductance gK]
        ENa[Battery ENa = +60 mV]
        EK[Battery EK = -90 mV]
        Eleak[Battery Eleak = -55 mV]
        ISTIM[Stimulus Current Istim]
    end

    Cm --- ISTIM
    Gna --- ENa
    Gk --- EK
    Rleak --- Eleak
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Saltatory Conduction in Myelinated Axon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Neuron Cell Body] --> B[Axon Hillock]
    B --> C[Myelin Sheath Segment 1]
    C --> D[Node of Ranvier 1 - AP regenerated]
    D --> E[Myelin Sheath Segment 2]
    E --> F[Node of Ranvier 2 - AP regenerated]
    F --> G[Axon Terminal]
    G --> H[Synaptic Cleft]
    H --> I[Postsynaptic Neuron]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological meaning:** Myelin acts as an insulator — the action potential effectively "jumps" (Latin: *saltare*) between Nodes of Ranvier where the density of voltage-gated Na⁺ channels is ~1000× higher than under myelin. This boosts conduction velocity from ~1 m/s (unmyelinated) to ~120 m/s (myelinated).

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ion Channel State Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Closed_Resting: at -70 mV
    Closed_Resting --> Open_Activated: depolarization to -55 mV
    Open_Activated --> Inactivated: within 1 ms
    Inactivated --> Closed_Resting: repolarization to -70 mV
    note right of Open_Activated: Na+ rushes IN
    note right of Inactivated: ABSOLUTE refractory period
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define resting membrane potential. Mention its typical value and the ion primarily responsible for it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **resting membrane potential (RMP)** is the steady-state voltage difference across the plasma membrane of a neuron when it is not transmitting a signal. The inside of the neuron is electrically negative relative to the outside.

- **Typical value:** **−70 mV** (range −60 to −90 mV across cell types).
- **Primary ion responsible:** **K⁺ (potassium)** — because at rest, the membrane is ~25× more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺. K⁺ leaks outward down its concentration gradient (inside: 140 mM → outside: 5 mM), leaving behind non-diffusible intracellular anions.

**Mark allocation:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for value, 1 mark for ion + mechanism.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between absolute and relative refractory periods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Absolute Refractory Period | Relative Refractory Period |
|---|---|---|
| Timing | During depolarization + most of repolarization | During late repolarization + hyperpolarization |
| Cause | Na⁺ channels are **inactivated** | Na⁺ channels recovered but K⁺ channels still open |
| New AP possible? | **No**, no stimulus — however strong | **Yes**, but requires a stronger-than-normal stimulus |
| Functional role | Enforces **one-way propagation** | Limits **maximum firing frequency** |

**Mark allocation:** 1.5 marks each for cause + functional consequence.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

## ✅ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

### (a) Derive the Nernst equation for a univalent ion. Calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM at 37 °C. (7 marks)

### (b) Explain the ionic basis of the action potential with a neat labeled diagram. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the physical setup — a semipermeable membrane separating two solutions of ion $X^{z+}$ at concentrations $[X]_{in}$ and $[X]_{out}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the electrochemical potential difference and apply equilibrium condition ($\Delta \mu = 0$). Show:

$$RT \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} + zF E_X = 0$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Rearrange and simplify using $\ln(a/b) = -\ln(b/a)$:

$$E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

Convert to base-10 form for body temperature:

$$E_X = \frac{61}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} \quad \text{(mV at 37 °C)}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute values for K⁺ ($z = +1$):

$$E_K = \frac{61}{1} \log_{10} \frac{5}{140}$$

$$E_K = 61 \times \log_{10}(0.0357) = 61 \times (-1.447)$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -88.3 \text{ mV}}$$

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Draw a clearly labeled voltage-vs-time graph of the action potential showing:
- Resting potential (−70 mV)
- Threshold (−55 mV)
- Peak (+30 to +40 mV)
- Hyperpolarization (−80 mV)
- Mark A, B, C phases.

**Step 2 (5 marks):** Explain each phase with ion channel states:

| Phase | Voltage Change | Channels |
|---|---|---|
| Depolarization | −70 → +30 mV | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels **open** rapidly → Na⁺ influx |
| Peak | +30 mV | Na⁺ channels **inactivate**; K⁺ channels fully open |
| Repolarization | +30 → −70 mV | K⁺ efflux dominates |
| Hyperpolarization | −70 → −80 mV | K⁺ channels close slowly; overshoot occurs |
| Recovery | −80 → −70 mV | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump restores ionic gradients (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Examiner's Warning:**
> Students often forget to mention that the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump is electrogenic** (net exports one positive charge per cycle). Without this, the RMP cannot be maintained over time. **Loss of 1 mark** if omitted.
> Also, students confuse "channel **inactivation**" with "channel **closure**" — they are *different* states! Inactivation is a distinct conformational state that prevents reopening even with depolarization.

---

## ✅ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

### (a) State and explain the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation. Why is it more realistic than the Nernst equation? (7 marks)

### (b) Discuss the refractory periods and their significance in nerve impulse conduction. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the GHK equation:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$$

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Explain each term:
- $V_m$ = membrane potential (V)
- $P_X$ = permeability of membrane to ion X (cm/s)
- $[X]_{in}$, $[X]_{out}$ = intracellular / extracellular concentrations (mM)
- The Cl⁻ term appears inverted because of its negative charge.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Explain realism:
- The **Nernst equation** considers only ONE permeant ion at a time.
- The **GHK equation** accounts for the fact that real membranes are simultaneously permeable to K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻ with different permeabilities that **change during the action potential** (e.g., $P_{Na}$ rises 600-fold at peak).
- GHK therefore accurately predicts the RMP (≈ −70 mV) under physiological conditions.

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define refractory period — the interval after one action potential during which a second action potential is difficult or impossible to elicit.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Absolute Refractory Period (ARP):
- Lasts ~1–2 ms; coincides with Na⁺ channel inactivation.
- No stimulus, regardless of strength, can elicit another AP.
- Ensures **unidirectional propagation** of the impulse.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Relative Refractory Period (RRP):
- Lasts a few ms after ARP; K⁺ channels still partially open.
- A stronger-than-normal stimulus can elicit an AP, but threshold is elevated.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Significance — Refractory periods:
1. Limit **maximum firing frequency** of a neuron (~500 Hz max).
2. Prevent **backward propagation** of the AP.
3. Enable **temporal coding** of stimulus intensity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students frequently write that refractory periods "prevent the neuron from being damaged." This is **incorrect** — they are functional constraints for signal fidelity, not protective mechanisms. **Loss of 2 marks** for this misconception.
> Also, do not confuse "absolute refractory period" with the entire AP duration; it is only ~half of it.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **RMP ≈ −70 mV** — set primarily by **K⁺ leak conductance** and maintained by **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase**.
- ✅ **Threshold ≈ −55 mV** — all-or-none principle; sub-threshold = graded, supra-threshold = AP.
- ✅ **AP peak ≈ +30 to +40 mV** — approaches but never reaches $E_{Na}$ (+60 mV) because Na⁺ channels inactivate before full equilibration.
- ✅ **Nernst equation:** $E_X = \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ mV at 37 °C.
- ✅ **GHK equation:** includes permeability weights for K⁺, Na⁺, Cl⁻ — more realistic.
- ✅ **Hodgkin-Huxley cycle:** Resting → Depolarization (Na⁺ in) → Repolarization (K⁺ out) → Hyperpolarization → Recovery.
- ✅ **Channel states:** Closed → Open → Inactivated (for Na⁺); Closed → Open (for K⁺).
- ✅ **Refractory periods:** Absolute (Na⁺ inactivated) → Relative (K⁺ still open).
- ✅ **Cable properties:** $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m/r_i}$; $\tau = R_m C_m$. Myelination ↑ $\lambda$.
- ✅ **Conduction velocity:** ~1 m/s (unmyelinated) → ~120 m/s (myelinated, saltatory).
- ✅ **Saltatory conduction:** AP regenerates only at **Nodes of Ranvier** (high Na⁺-channel density).
- ✅ **Numerical constants:** RT/F ≈ 26.7 mV at 37 °C; 2.303 × RT/F ≈ 61 mV.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subtypes of Ion Channels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT752 - NEUROBIOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Subtypes of Ion Channels ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **ion channel** is an integral transmembrane protein (typically a multimer of α-subunits with auxiliary β-subunits) that forms a water-filled, ion-selective pore through the lipid bilayer. Channels operate on the principle of **electrochemical gradients** and may exist in multiple conformational states — *closed (resting)*, *open (activated)*, and *inactivated*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):** > Ion channel subtypes are structurally and functionally distinct families of pore-forming membrane proteins classified on the basis of their **gating stimulus (activation mechanism)**, **ion selectivity (permeability)**, and **kinetic behavior (activation/inactivation rates)**. ### 1.2 Major Subtypes — Overview | # | Subtype | Gating Stimulus | Example Ions | Example Channel | |---|---------|-----------------|--------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Voltage-Gated (VGICs)** | Change in membrane potential (ΔVₘ) | Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻ | Naᵥ1.1, Kᵥ2.1, Caᵥ1.1 | | 2 | **Ligand-Gated (LGICs / iGluRs)** | Binding of chemical ligand | Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺ | nAChR, GABA-A, NMDA | | 3 | **Mechanically-Gated** | Membrane stretch / shear | Na⁺, Ca²⁺, K⁺ | Piezo1, TRPV4 | | 4 | **Leak / Background Channels** | Constitutively open (no gating) | K⁺ (K2P family), Na⁺ | TREK-1, TASK-1 | | 5 | **Second-Messenger Gated** | cAMP, cGMP, Ca²⁺, G-protein | Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺ | HCN, CNG, BK, SK | | 6 | **Gap Junction Channels** | Direct cytoplasmic coupling | Ions ≤ 1 kDa | Connexin-43, Pannexin-1 | | 7 | **ATP-Gated** | Extracellular ATP | Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺ | P2X1–P2X7 | | 8 | **Inwardly Rectifying (Kir)** | Block by intracellular Mg²⁺ / polyamines | K⁺ | Kir2.1, Kir3.1 (GIRK) | | 9 | **Temperature-Gated (ThermoTRP)** | Temperature change | Na⁺, Ca²⁺, K⁺ | TRPV1 (≥ 43°C), TRPM8 (≤ 25°C) | | 10 | **Store-Operated (SOCs)** | ER Ca²⁺ depletion | Ca²⁺ | Orai1, STIM1 | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Water-Tap" Model > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine the cell membrane as a **thick concrete wall** separating two reservoirs of water. The ion channels are **smart taps** punched through this wall. Each tap is built for *one* job: > - A **voltage-gated tap** opens only when a sensor detects that the *water pressure* on one side has changed by a specific amount. > - A **ligand-gated tap** opens only when a *specific key* (neurotransmitter) is inserted. > - A **mechanically-gated tap** opens when the wall is *pushed or stretched*. > - A **leak tap** is **always open**, allowing a steady, slow trickle that sets the resting level. > > Just as taps have filters (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻), channels have a **selectivity filter** that decides which ion passes. ### 1.4 Physical & Biophysical Constants Used in Channel Biophysics > **[!IMPORTANT] Standard Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:** > - **Faraday's Constant, F = 96,485 C/mol** (≈ 9.648 × 10⁴ C/mol) > - **Gas Constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** > - **Body Temperature (KTU standard), T = 310 K (37°C)** > - **Universal Gas Constant Product, RT/F ≈ 26.71 mV** at 37°C > - **Elementary Charge, e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** > - **Avogadro's Number, N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ /mol** > - **Boltzmann Constant, k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** > - **Molar Conductance of K⁺ at 25°C, λ⁰(K⁺) ≈ 73.5 S·cm²/mol** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification Logic (Step-by-Step) **Step 1 — Identify the Gating Stimulus** The first decision in classifying a channel is *what* makes it open. The options are: 1. **Voltage** (ΔVₘ) → Voltage-Gated 2. **Ligand** (neurotransmitter, ATP) → Ligand-Gated 3. **Mechanical force** (stretch, shear) → Mechanically-Gated 4. **Metabolite / second messenger** (Ca²⁺, cAMP, cGMP) → Second-Messenger Gated 5. **Constitutive** (no stimulus) → Leak Channels **Step 2 — Identify the Permeant Ion (Selectivity)** Channels are named for the ion they pass most efficiently. Selectivity arises from the **selectivity filter** — a narrow constriction (often the P-loop or P-helix) lined with backbone carbonyls or specific residues (e.g., the **DEKA ring** of Na⁺ channels, the **TVGYG signature** of K⁺ channels). **Step 3 — Identify the Kinetic Behavior** - **Fast-activating, fast-inactivating** (e.g., Naᵥ) → mediate action-potential *upstroke* - **Slow-activating, slow-inactivating** (e.g., Kᵥ) → mediate *repolarization* - **Non-inactivating** (e.g., K₂P, HCN) → set *resting potential / Ih* - **Calcium-dependent** (e.g., BK, SK) → *after-hyperpolarization* **Step 4 — The "Why" — Functional Role** | Channel | Functional Role in Neuron | |---------|---------------------------| | Voltage-gated Na⁺ | Action potential initiation & propagation | | Voltage-gated K⁺ | Repolarization, spike-frequency adaptation | | Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ | Neurotransmitter release at terminal | | Ligand-gated (nAChR) | Fast excitatory synaptic transmission (NMJ) | | Ligand-gated (GABA-A) | Fast inhibitory synaptic transmission | | HCN ("pacemaker") | Sag, resonance, dendritic integration | | Leak K⁺ (K₂P) | Sets resting Vₘ near E_K | | Inwardly rectifying K⁺ | Stabilizes resting Vₘ, terminates excitation | ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and standard across Purves, Kandel, and Hille textbooks. | # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---------------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | **Nernst Equation** (equilibrium potential) | $E_{\text{ion}} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{o}}{[\text{ion}]_{i}}$ | E_ion in volts; z = ion valence; concentrations in mol/L | | 2 | **Nernst (log₁₀ form, 37°C)** | $E_{\text{ion}} \approx \dfrac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{o}}{[\text{ion}]_{i}}$ | Quick clinical form | | 3 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage** | $V_{m} = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{o} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{o} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{i}}{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{i} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{i} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{o}}$ | Pᵢ = permeability coefficient | | 4 | **Single-Channel Current (Ohm's law)** | $i = g \cdot (V_{m} - E_{\text{rev}})$ | g = single-channel conductance (pS); E_rev = reversal potential | | 5 | **Macroscopic Current** | $I = N \cdot P_{o} \cdot g \cdot (V_{m} - E_{\text{rev}})$ | N = number of channels; P_o = open probability | | 6 | **Hodgkin-Huxley Na⁺ current** | $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} \, m^{3} h \, (V_{m} - E_{Na})$ | m = activation; h = inactivation | | 7 | **Hodgkin-Huxley K⁺ current** | $I_{K} = \bar{g}_{K} \, n^{4} \, (V_{m} - E_{K})$ | n = activation gate | | 8 | **Chord Conductance** | $g = \dfrac{I}{V_{m} - E_{\text{rev}}}$ | Instantaneous measure | | 9 | **Q₁₀ (Temperature coefficient)** | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}\right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_{2}-T_{1}}}$ | R = reaction rate; T in °C | | 10 | **GHK Current (single ion)** | $I_{ion} = P_{ion} \, z^{2} \, \dfrac{F^{2} V_{m}}{RT} \cdot \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{i} - [\text{ion}]_{o} \, e^{-zFV_{m}/RT}}{1 - e^{-zFV_{m}/RT}}$ | Microscopic form | | 11 | **Reversal Potential from GHK** | $E_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{\sum P_{c}[C]_{o} + \sum P_{a}[A]_{i}}{\sum P_{c}[C]_{i} + \sum P_{a}[A]_{o}}$ | C=cations, A=anions | | 12 | **Cable Equation (length constant)** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_{m}}{r_{i}}}$ | r_m = membrane resistivity; r_i = axial resistivity | | 13 | **Time Constant τ** | $\tau = r_{m} \cdot c_{m}$ | r_m in Ω·cm²; c_m in F/cm² | | 14 | **Mean Open Time** | $\langle \tau_{o} \rangle = \dfrac{1}{\alpha + \beta}$ | α, β = rate constants (open/close) | | 15 | **Deactivation Time** | $\tau_{\text{deact}} = \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ | β = closing rate constant | ### 2.3 Engineering Utility (Real-World Relevance) > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters beyond neuroscience:** > - **Pharmacology:** ~60% of current drug targets are ion channels (e.g., **lidocaine** blocks Naᵥ, **amlodipine** blocks Caᵥ1, **diazepam** modulates GABA-A). > - **Toxicology:** Tetrodotoxin (pufferfish), saxitoxin (red tide), and dendrotoxin all target specific ion-channel subtypes — KTU 2024 emphasizes these for clinical neurology. > - **Cardiac Electrophysiology:** HCN ("funny") channels in SA node → **ivabradine** target for heart-rate reduction. > - **Synthetic Biology & Biosensors:** Engineered ligand-gated channels (PSAM, PSEM) are used in **chemogenetics**. > - **Neural Prosthetics:** Voltage-gated channels underlie every cochlear-implant and retinal-stimulator electrode design. ### 2.4 Detailed Architecture of a Voltage-Gated Na⁺ Channel (α-Subunit) - **24 transmembrane segments** organized as **4 homologous domains (DI–DIV)**, each with **6 helices (S1–S6)**. - **S1–S4 = Voltage-Sensor Domain (VSD)** — S4 carries **gating charges** (positively charged Arg/Lys residues). - **S5–S6 = Pore-Forming Domain (PFD)** — contains the **P-loop / P-helix / selectivity filter**. - **S4–S5 linker** couples VSD movement to pore opening. - **IFM motif (Ile-Phe-Met)** on the **DIII–DIV linker** mediates **fast inactivation** (the "ball-and-chain" or "hinged-lid" mechanism). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation #1 — The Nernst Equation (Equilibrium Potential of a Single Ion) **Step 1: Start with electrochemical potential equality at equilibrium** At equilibrium, the **chemical (osmotic) potential difference** across the membrane must equal the **electrical potential difference**. Otherwise, net ion flow would continue (violating equilibrium). $$\mu_{i} = \mu_{o} \quad \text{(at equilibrium)}$$ **Step 2: Expand chemical and electrical potential** For an ion `X` of valence `z`: $$\mu^{0} + RT \ln [X]_{i} + zF V_{i} = \mu^{0} + RT \ln [X]_{o} + zF V_{o}$$ The standard chemical potential `μ⁰` cancels because it is identical on both sides. **Step 3: Isolate the voltage difference** $$zF V_{i} - zF V_{o} = RT \ln [X]_{o} - RT \ln [X]_{i}$$ $$zF (V_{i} - V_{o}) = RT \ln \dfrac{[X]_{o}}{[X]_{i}}$$ **Step 4: Define E_ion = V_i − V_o (the equilibrium / Nernst potential)** $$E_{\text{ion}} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[X]_{o}}{[X]_{i}}$$ This is the **Nernst Equation**. **Step 5: Convert natural log → log₁₀ using ln(x) = 2.303 · log₁₀(x)** $$E_{\text{ion}} = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \dfrac{[X]_{o}}{[X]_{i}}$$ **Step 6: At 37°C (T = 310 K), evaluate the constant** $$\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$ Therefore, the **physiologist's form** at body temperature is: $$E_{\text{ion}} \approx \dfrac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[X]_{o}}{[X]_{i}}$$ > **[!NOTE] Sanity Check for K⁺:** With [K⁺]ₒ = 4 mM, [K⁺]ᵢ = 140 mM, z = +1 > $E_K = (61/1) \log_{10}(4/140) = 61 \cdot (-1.544) \approx -94 \text{ mV}$ ✓ (matches textbook resting-permeability range) ### 3.2 Derivation #2 — The GHK Voltage Equation (Multi-Ion Resting Potential) **Step 1: Use the constant-field assumption (Goldman 1943)** Assume the membrane's electric field is **constant** across its thickness; the ion flux `J` for ion `i` becomes: $$J_{i} = P_{i} z_{i}^{2} \dfrac{F^{2} V_{m}}{RT} \cdot \dfrac{[i]_{i} - [i]_{o} \, e^{-z_{i} F V_{m}/RT}}{1 - e^{-z_{i} F V_{m}/RT}}$$ **Step 2: At steady state (resting Vₘ), net charge flux = 0** For monovalent ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻), the constraint of electroneutrality on net current is: $$J_{Na} + J_{K} - J_{Cl} = 0$$ **Step 3: Substitute the constant-field flux for each ion, simplify, and solve for Vₘ** After algebraic simplification (with z = +1 for Na⁺, K⁺ and z = −1 for Cl⁻), one obtains: $$V_{m} = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{o} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{o} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{i}}{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{i} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{i} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{o}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight:** Notice that **Cl⁻** appears with the **inside** concentration in the numerator and **outside** in the denominator — this is because its valence is **negative** (`z = −1`), which inverts the ratio. Students often miss this sign and lose full marks. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Properties of Major Ion-Channel Subtypes | Property | Naᵥ (Voltage-gated Na⁺) | Kᵥ (Voltage-gated K⁺) | Caᵥ (Voltage-gated Ca²⁺) | nAChR (LGIC) | GABA-A (LGIC) | HCN (Pacemaker) | K₂P (Leak) | |----------|-------------------------|------------------------|---------------------------|---------------|---------------|-----------------|-----------| | Gating stimulus | Depolarization | Depolarization | Depolarization | ACh (2 mol) | GABA (2 mol) | Hyperpolarization + cAMP | Constitutive | | Selectivity | Na⁺ (P_Na/P_K ≈ 12:1) | K⁺ (P_K/P_Na ≈ 100:1) | Ca²⁺ (P_Ca/P_Na ≈ 1000:1) | Na⁺, K⁺ (non-selective cation) | Cl⁻ (selective) | Na⁺, K⁺ (mixed) | K⁺ | | Single-channel conductance (γ) | **~10–20 pS** | **~5–20 pS** | **~1–25 pS** | **~30–50 pS** | **~10–30 pS** | **~1–2 pS** | **~5–60 pS** | | Activation | Fast (ms) | Slow (ms–s) | Slow | Fast (µs–ms) | Fast (ms) | Slow (s) | N/A | | Inactivation | Fast (h gate, ~ms) | Slow (minutes) | Voltage + Ca²⁺-dependent | Desensitization (s) | Desensitization (s) | None / minimal | None | | Reversal potential (E_rev) | **+50 to +60 mV** | **−90 to −100 mV** | **+120 to +130 mV** | **~0 mV** | **~−70 mV** | **~−25 to −40 mV** | **~−90 mV** | | Key blocker | **Tetrodotoxin (TTX)**, lidocaine | **Tetraethylammonium (TEA)**, 4-AP | **ω-conotoxin GVIA**, nifedipine | **α-bungarotoxin**, curare | **Picrotoxin**, bicuculline | **ZD7288**, ivabradine | **Barium (Ba²⁺)**, norfluoxetine | | Functional role | AP upstroke | AP repolarization, AHP | Neurotransmitter release | Fast EPSC (NMJ) | Fast IPSC | Sag, resonance, pacemaking | Sets RMP | ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **[!IMPORTANT] Worked Problem:** > A neuron has the following internal and external ion concentrations: [K⁺]ᵢ = 140 mM, [K⁺]ₒ = 4 mM; [Na⁺]ᵢ = 12 mM, [Na⁺]ₒ = 145 mM; [Cl⁻]ᵢ = 4 mM, [Cl⁻]ₒ = 120 mM. Permeabilities (relative) at rest: P_K : P_Na : P_Cl = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45. Body temperature = 37°C. Calculate (a) the Nernst potentials for K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻, and (b) the resting membrane potential using the GHK equation. **Solution (a) — Nernst Potentials:** For K⁺ (z = +1): $$E_K = 61 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4}{140}\right) = 61 \times (-1.544) = -94.2 \text{ mV}$$ For Na⁺ (z = +1): $$E_{Na} = 61 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{145}{12}\right) = 61 \times (1.082) = +66.0 \text{ mV}$$ For Cl⁻ (z = −1): $$E_{Cl} = -\dfrac{61}{1} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{120}{4}\right) = -61 \times (1.477) = -90.1 \text{ mV}$$ **Solution (b) — GHK Resting Vₘ:** $$V_{m} = 61 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{(1)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1)(140) + (0.04)(12) + (0.45)(120)}\right) \text{ mV}$$ Numerator: $$4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$$ Denominator: $$140 + 0.48 + 54 = 194.48$$ $$V_{m} = 61 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{11.6}{194.48}\right) = 61 \times (-1.224) = -74.7 \text{ mV}$$ > **[!NOTE] KTU Examiner Note:** This value (~−75 mV) is the **expected answer** for a typical mammalian neuron. Always show all three Nernst calculations first — full marks require the *preliminary* E_ion values before the GHK substitution. ## 4.
