KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]** **Differentiate between voltage-gated and ligand-gated ion channels. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (≈ 100 words):** Voltage-gated ion channels (VGICs) open in response to changes in the transmembrane potential (ΔVₘ); their activation is governed by voltage-sensor domains (S4 helices carrying gating charges). Examples: Naᵥ (Naᵥ1.1), Kᵥ, Caᵥ. Ligand-gated ion channels (LGICs) open when a specific chemical messenger (neurotransmitter or ATP) binds to an extracellular or intracellular domain. Examples: nAChR (cation-selective, excitatory), GABA-A (Cl⁻-selective, inhibitory), NMDA receptor. Key distinctions include the *gating stimulus* (voltage vs chemical), *kinetics* (VGICs are typically slower, ms range; LGICs can be sub-millisecond), and *physiological role* (VGICs mediate action potentials, LGICs mediate fast synaptic transmission). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Repeated Pattern]** **Explain the role of HCN channels in neuronal excitability.** **Model Answer (≈ 100 words):** HCN (Hyperpolarization-activated Cyclic Nucleotide-gated) channels are non-selective cation channels that *open upon hyperpolarization* and conduct a mixed Na⁺/K⁺ current called **Iₕ** (or I_f in cardiac pacemaker cells). Their reversal potential is ~ −25 to −40 mV. Functionally, Iₕ: - Produces the **"sag"** in membrane potential during sustained hyperpolarization. - Contributes to **resonance** and **dendritic integration** by opposing hyperpolarization. - Sets the **resting Vₘ** in many pacemaker neurons. - Is **modulated by cAMP** binding (cyclic-nucleotide binding domain at the C-terminus) — increased cAMP shifts activation curves to more positive potentials. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[!NOTE] [KTU ESE Pattern — Module 2 Internal Choice, July 2024 Style]** > **(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the **molecular architecture of a voltage-gated Na⁺ channel** and explain the role of the **S4 voltage sensor**. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for an ion `X` of valence `z`. Using a sample dataset, calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37°C. **(7 marks)** --- **(a) Model Answer — Architecture of Naᵥ Channel (7 marks):** 1. The **α-subunit** is a single polypeptide of **~2000 amino acids**, organized into **4 homologous domains (DI–DIV)**. Each domain contains **6 transmembrane α-helices (S1–S6)** — total **24 TM segments**. 2. The **S1–S4 helices** of each domain constitute the **Voltage-Sensor Domain (VSD)**. S4 contains **4–8 positively charged residues** (Arg/Lys) acting as gating charges. 3. The **S5–S6 helices** of each domain form the **Pore-Forming Domain (PFD)**. The **P-loop (SS1–SS2)** between S5 and S6 dips into the membrane and forms the **selectivity filter**. 4. The four P-loops (one from each domain) come together to form a single central pore. The **DEKA ring** (Asp, Glu, Lys, Ala) on the four P-loops confers **Na⁺ selectivity over K⁺**. 5. The **DIII–DIV intracellular linker** contains the **IFM motif** (Ile-Phe-Met), which acts as a **fast inactivation gate** by docking into the inner mouth of the pore (the "hinged-lid" mechanism). 6. The **S4–S5 linker** couples the VSD motion to the pore opening. 7. **Auxiliary β-subunits (β1–β4)** modulate kinetics, trafficking, and pharmacology. **Role of S4 voltage sensor:** During depolarization, the S4 helices undergo an **outward, helical screw-like movement** (sliding 5–8 Å outward), driven by the electric field. This **translocation of gating charges** (the "gating current") pulls on the S4–S5 linker and opens the pore. The Q/R site–type "gating pore" current can also pass through defective S4 segments (relevant in hypokalemic periodic paralysis). --- **(b) Model Answer — Derivation & Numerical Calculation of Nernst Potential (7 marks):** **Derivation (5 marks):** - Start from equilibrium of electrochemical potential: `μᵢ = μₒ` → expand → isolate voltage → obtain the Nernst equation. - Show the conversion from `ln` to `log₁₀` and substitute the constant `2.303 RT/F ≈ 61 mV` at 37°C. (See Section 3.1 above for the full step-by-step derivation.) **Numerical calculation (2 marks):** Sample dataset: [K⁺]ₒ = 4 mM, [K⁺]ᵢ = 140 mM, z = +1, T = 37°C. $$E_K = \dfrac{61 \text{ mV}}{1} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4}{140}\right) = 61 \times (-1.544) = -94.2 \text{ mV}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** > **[!NOTE] [KTU ESE Pattern — Alternative Choice, Module 2, July 2024 Style]** > **(a)** Classify ion channels based on their **gating mechanism** and give a one-line description with example for each subtype. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** What is the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation**? How does it differ from the Nernst equation? Using typical mammalian neuronal ion concentrations, compute the resting membrane potential with relative permeabilities P_K : P_Na : P_Cl = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45. **(7 marks)** --- **(a) Model Answer — Classification of Ion Channels by Gating (7 marks):** A clean **tabular form** earns full marks: | Class | Gating Stimulus | Example | Permeant Ions | |-------|-----------------|---------|---------------| | **Voltage-Gated (VGICs)** | ΔVₘ | Naᵥ, Kᵥ, Caᵥ | Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺ | | **Ligand-Gated (LGICs / iGluRs)** | Neurotransmitter binding | nAChR, GABA-A, NMDA | Na⁺/K⁺, Cl⁻, Na⁺/K⁺/Ca²⁺ | | **Mechanically-Gated** | Membrane stretch, shear | Piezo1, TRPV4 | Na⁺, Ca²⁺ | | **Second-Messenger Gated** | cAMP, cGMP, Ca²⁺, G-protein | HCN, CNG, BK, SK | Na⁺/K⁺, K⁺ | | **Leak / Background** | Constitutively open | TREK-1, TASK-1 (K₂P family) | K⁺ | | **Gap Junctions** | Direct cytoplasmic continuity | Connexin-43 | Ions ≤ 1 kDa | | **Inwardly Rectifying** | Mg²⁺ / polyamine block | Kir2.1, Kir3.1 (GIRK) | K⁺ | | **ATP-Gated** | Extracellular ATP | P2X1–P2X7 | Na⁺, Ca²⁺ | | **ThermoTRP** | Temperature | TRPV1 (≥ 43°C), TRPM8 (≤ 25°C) | Na⁺, Ca²⁺ | --- **(b) Model Answer — GHK Equation and Computation (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** The GHK (Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz) voltage equation gives the **membrane potential at steady state** when the membrane is permeable to **multiple ions simultaneously**, assuming a constant electric field across the membrane: $$V_{m} = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{o} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{o} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{i}}{P_{K}[K^{+}]_{i} + P_{Na}[Na^{+}]_{i} + P_{Cl}[Cl^{-}]_{o}}$$ **Difference from Nernst (2 marks):** - **Nernst** considers **a single ion** at equilibrium; it gives the **equilibrium potential of one ion species** (E_ion). - **GHK** integrates **multiple ions weighted by their permeabilities** to predict the **actual resting Vₘ** under steady-state, non-equilibrium conditions. - GHK predicts Vₘ closer to the ion with the **highest permeability**; if P_K ≫ P_Na, Vₘ → E_K. **Numerical computation (3 marks):** (See Section 3.4 Worked Example above.) With [K⁺]ᵢ = 140, [K⁺]ₒ = 4, [Na⁺]ᵢ = 12, [Na⁺]ₒ = 145, [Cl⁻]ᵢ = 4, [Cl⁻]ₒ = 120, and P_K:P_Na:P_Cl = 1:0.04:0.45: $$V_m \approx -74.7 \text{ mV}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Board Examiners' Red-Flag List):** > 1. **Sign of z in Nernst equation:** Students forget to invert the ratio for **Cl⁻ (z = −1)**. For Cl⁻, the formula must be $E_{Cl} = -\dfrac{61}{-1} \log_{10}([Cl]_o/[Cl]_i) = -61 \log_{10}(120/4) \approx -90$ mV, **not** +90 mV. > 2. **GHK vs Nernst confusion:** Many students write the GHK equation but compute the Nernst potential — you will get **zero** for the Vₘ calculation if you skip the multi-ion weighting. > 3. **Inverting the Cl⁻ ratio in GHK numerator/denominator:** As noted in Section 3.2, Cl⁻ appears as `[Cl⁻]ᵢ` in the numerator and `[Cl⁻]ₒ` in the denominator, *opposite* to cations. Easy to lose 2 marks here. > 4. **Mixing up channel families:** Writing "K_v is a leak channel" or "GABA-A is excitatory" — these are conceptually wrong; examiners deduct **2 full marks**. > 5. **Forgetting the temperature constant 61 mV** — must explicitly state **T = 37°C (310 K)** in derivations. Using the 58 mV constant (25°C) without correction loses marks. > 6. **Naming channels without stating the permeant ion:** "K_v channel" alone is *not enough*; write "voltage-gated K⁺ channel" or "Kᵥ1.x family". --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **[!IMPORTANT] Rapid-Revision Checklist — Subtypes of Ion Channels (Module 2):** > - ✅ Ion channels are classified by **gating stimulus** (voltage, ligand, mechanical, second-messenger) and by **ion selectivity** (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻, non-selective). > - ✅ **Voltage-gated channels** have **4 (or 24) TM helices**, S4 carries **gating charges**, S5–S6 form the **pore**, and the **P-loop** houses the **selectivity filter**. > - ✅ **Naᵥ** channels have a **fast inactivation gate** via the **IFM motif** (hinged-lid) on the **DIII–DIV linker**. > - ✅ **Ligand-gated channels** (LGICs) are the fastest signal transducers — **nAChR** (excitatory) and **GABA-A** (inhibitory) are the two classic examples; both can desensitize. > - ✅ **HCN channels** open on **hyperpolarization**, conduct **Iₕ**, contribute to the **sag**, and are **cAMP-modulated**. > - ✅ **Leak K⁺ channels (K₂P)** are constitutively open and **set the resting Vₘ near E_K**. > - ✅ **Inwardly rectifying K⁺ (Kir) channels** pass K⁺ better *into* the cell at hyperpolarized Vₘ; blocked by intracellular **Mg²⁺ and polyamines**. > - ✅ **Mechanically-gated** channels (**Piezo1/2**) sense **stretch** and mediate **touch, proprioception, hearing**. > - ✅ **Gap junction channels** (connexin-43) couple adjacent cells electrically, allowing ions and second messengers up to **~1 kDa** to pass. > - ✅ The **Nernst equation** gives the equilibrium potential for a single ion: $E_{ion} = (RT/zF) \ln([ion]_o/[ion]_i)$. > - ✅ The **GHK equation** gives the actual Vₘ when multiple ions are permeable: $V_m = (RT/F) \ln \dfrac{\sum P_c [C]_o + \sum P_a [A]_i}{\sum P_c [C]_i + \sum P_a [A]_o}$. > - ✅ At 37°C, **2.303 RT/F ≈ 61 mV** — always use this constant for mammalian neurons. > - ✅ Typical resting Vₘ of a mammalian neuron ≈ **−70 to −75 mV** (from the GHK computation). > - ✅ Classic pharmacology: **TTX → Naᵥ**; **TEA, 4-AP → Kᵥ**; **ω-conotoxin → Caᵥ (N-type)**; **picrotoxin → GABA-A**; **α-bungarotoxin → nAChR**. > - ✅ **Conductance g = I/(Vₘ − E_rev)** — the chord-conductance formula is **exam-favorite**. > - ✅ **Hodgkin-Huxley formalism:** $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na})$ and $I_{K} = \bar{g}_{K} n^4 (V - E_{K})$ — know the role of *m* (activation), *h* (Na⁺ inactivation), and *n* (K⁺ activation).

topics.title: Membrane Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT752 - NEUROBIOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Membrane Potential ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Membrane Potential ($V_m$)**: The electrical potential difference (voltage) that exists across the plasma membrane of a neuron (or any excitable cell), measured as the inside of the cell relative to the outside. It is generated by the unequal distribution of ions across the lipid bilayer and the selective permeability of the membrane to those ions. Mathematically stated: $$ V_m = V_{in} - V_{out} $$ By KTU 2024 convention, the **resting membrane potential** of a typical mammalian neuron is approximately **$-70\ \text{mV}$** (millivolts), meaning the intracellular side is electrically negative with respect to the extracellular side. ### 1.2 Sub-Categories of Membrane Potential | Term | Definition | Typical Value | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Resting Potential** | Stable $V_m$ when the neuron is *not* firing | $-70\ \text{mV}$ | | **Equilibrium Potential ($E_{ion}$)** | Voltage at which net ionic flux = 0 (Nernst) | $-90$ to $+60\ \text{mV}$ | | **Threshold Potential** | $V_m$ value that *triggers* an action potential | $-55\ \text{mV}$ | | **Action Potential Peak** | Maximum depolarization during firing | $+30$ to $+40\ \text{mV}$ | | **After-Hyperpolarization** | Undershoot below resting $V_m$ | $-80\ \text{mV}$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight**: Students must clearly distinguish between *Equilibrium Potential* (calculated for a *single* ion via the Nernst equation) and *Resting Membrane Potential* (the *actual* $V_m$ of the cell, which is a *weighted blend* of several ions, given by the Goldman equation). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Charged Battery Inside a Leaky Pipe" Imagine a **water tank (the neuron)** separated from a **reservoir (the extracellular fluid)** by a **semi-permeable rubber sheet (the membrane)**. * The rubber sheet has **tiny holes (ion channels)** that allow some salts to leak through, but not others. * Inside the tank, we deliberately pour **potassium-rich salt water** (high $[K^+]_{in}$), while the reservoir has **sodium-rich salt water** (high $[Na^+]_{out}$). * Because the holes let **$K^+$ leak out faster than $Na^+$ leaks in**, positive charge slowly escapes, leaving the inside **negatively charged** — exactly like a rechargeable battery storing energy across two terminals. * A **pump (the $Na^+/K^+$ ATPase)** continuously pushes the leaked ions back, keeping the battery "charged" at $-70\ \text{mV}$. > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Takeaway**: Membrane potential is *not* a single event — it is a **dynamic steady-state** maintained by three simultaneous players: **(1) Ion concentration gradients, (2) Selective permeability of channels, and (3) Active pumping by $Na^+/K^+$ ATPase**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants (Must Be Memorized) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following constants appear in **every KTU numerical question** on this topic. Commit them to memory. * **Faraday's Constant** $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol} \approx \mathbf{9.65 \times 10^4\ C\cdot mol^{-1}}$ * **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol}\cdot\text{K)}$ * **Body Temperature** $T = 310\ \text{K}$ ($37^\circ\text{C}$) * **Thermal Voltage Factor** $\dfrac{RT}{F} = 26.7\ \text{mV}$ (natural log) or $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = \mathbf{61.5\ mV}$ (log₁₀) at $37^\circ\text{C}$. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Membrane Potential #### **Pillar 1 — Ion Concentration Gradients (Chemical Driving Force)** The Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump establishes steep ionic gradients across the membrane. Typical mammalian neuronal values: | Ion | Intracellular $[\text{mM}]$ | Extracellular $[\text{mM}]$ | Gradient Ratio (Out/In) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $K^+$ | 140 | 5 | 0.036 (high inside) | | $Na^+$ | 10 | 145 | 14.5 (high outside) | | $Cl^-$ | 4 | 120 | 30 (high outside) | | $Ca^{2+}$ | $10^{-4}$ | 1.5 | 15000 (extremely high outside) | | $A^-$ (Proteins) | 138 | ~0 | Essentially impermeable | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters**: Ions diffuse *down* their concentration gradient. For $K^+$, that means **outward**; for $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$, that means **inward**. The direction of net movement is dictated purely by chemistry. #### **Pillar 2 — Selective Permeability (Electrical Driving Force)** The lipid bilayer is intrinsically impermeable to ions. Movement requires **transmembrane proteins**: * **Leak Channels** — Always open; non-gated. * **Voltage-Gated Channels** — Open/close in response to $V_m$ change. * **Ligand-Gated Channels** — Open upon neurotransmitter binding. * **Mechanically-Gated Channels** — Open upon physical deformation. > [!IMPORTANT] > At **rest**, neuronal membranes are **~25× more permeable to $K^+$** than to $Na^+$. This single fact is why resting $V_m$ ($-70\ \text{mV}$) lies *close* to $E_K$ ($-90\ \text{mV}$) and *far* from $E_{Na}$ ($+60\ \text{mV}$). #### **Pillar 3 — Active Pumping (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase)** A **P-type ATPase** electrogenic pump uses **1 ATP** to export **3 Na⁺** out and import **2 K⁺** in. $$ 3\,Na^+_{in} + 2\,K^+_{out} + 1\,ATP \xrightarrow{\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+ \text{ ATPase}} 3\,Na^+_{out} + 2\,K^+_{in} + ADP + P_i $$ * **Electrogenic**: Net transfer of **+1 charge out** per cycle → directly contributes $\sim -3$ to $-5\ \text{mV}$ to $V_m$. * Maintains the gradients that the Nernst and Goldman equations *assume are constant*. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Master this table. Every KTU Part-B question on Module 2 will require **at least 2 of these equations**. | # | Equation Name | Formula | Key Variables / Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Nernst Equation** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | $z$ = ion valence; result in Volts | | 2 | **Nernst (log₁₀ form, 37 °C)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\ \text{mV}$ | Direct $mV$ result | | 3 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage** | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$ | $P_{ion}$ = permeability (cm/s) | | 4 | **GHK (log₁₀ form)** | $V_m = 61.5\log_{10}\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\ \text{mV}$ | Direct $mV$ result | | 5 | **Nernst-Planck Flux** | $J = -D\left(\dfrac{dC}{dx} + \dfrac{zFC}{RT}\dfrac{dV}{dx}\right)$ | $J$ = flux; $D$ = diffusion coeff. | | 6 | **Capacitance Relation** | $Q = C_m V_m$ | $C_m \approx 1\ \mu\text{F/cm}^2$ | | 7 | **Time Constant** | $\tau = R_m C_m$ | Membrane time constant ($\sim 10\ \text{ms}$) | | 8 | **GHK Current Density** | $I_{ion} = P_{ion}\dfrac{z^2 F^2 V_m}{RT}\dfrac{[ion]_i - [ion]_o e^{-zFV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-zFV_m/RT}}$ | Used in Hodgkin-Huxley model | ### 2.3 Engineering / Real-World Utility * **Electrophysiology & EEG/ECoG**: Clinical neurology and brain-computer interfaces rely entirely on measuring $V_m$ deviations. * **Pharmacology**: Most anesthetic, antiepileptic, and cardiac drugs (e.g., lidocaine, amiodarone) work by **modifying ion channel permeability** — which directly shifts $V_m$. * **Cardiac Pacemakers**: Artificial pacemakers replicate the rhythmic depolarization maintained by membrane potential oscillation in SA-node cells. * **Neural Prosthetics**: Cochlear implants and retinal prosthetics *replace* defective $V_m$ signaling with externally-driven electrical stimuli. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Nernst Equation (from Thermodynamic Equilibrium) **Premise**: At electrochemical equilibrium, the *chemical potential gradient* (driving diffusion) is exactly balanced by the *electrical potential gradient* (driving drift). **Step 1 — Chemical Potential Difference Across the Membrane** The molar Gibbs free energy of an ion on the inside vs outside: $$ \Delta G = RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}} $$ *This is the energy change (per mole) when an ion moves from out → in against its concentration gradient.* **Step 2 — Electrical Work Done Moving Charge $zF$** If the inside is at potential $V$ relative to outside, the electrical work to move $1$ mole of ions with valence $z$ from outside to inside: $$ W_{elec} = z F V_m $$ **Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition: $\Delta G + W_{elec} = 0$** At equilibrium, the *total* free energy change vanishes (no net flux): $$ RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}} + z F V_m = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $V_m$** Rearrange to isolate $V_m$: $$ z F V_m = -RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}} = RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{V_m = E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}} $$ This is the **Nernst Equation**. Converting to log₁₀ (multiplies numerator by $2.303$): $$ \boxed{E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}} $$ At $37^\circ\text{C}$, $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 61.5\ \text{mV}$, yielding the *memorized* form: $$ E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\ \text{mV} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Goldman Equation (Constant-Field Assumption) **Premise**: The membrane has a *constant electric field* across it (linear potential drop), and *multiple* ions contribute to $V_m$. **Step 1 — Start with the Nernst-Planck Equation (Flux form)** For a single ion with constant field $E = V_m/d$: $$ J_{ion} = -D_{ion}\left(\dfrac{d[ion]}{dx} + \dfrac{zF[ion]}{RT}\dfrac{V_m}{d}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Integrate Across the Membrane** (from $x=0$ to $x=d$) After integrating with boundary conditions $[ion](0) = [ion]_{in}$ and $[ion](d) = [ion]_{out}$, the net flux simplifies to: $$ J_{ion} = P_{ion}\dfrac{z^2 F^2 V_m}{RT}\dfrac{[ion]_i - [ion]_o\, e^{-zFV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-zFV_m/RT}} $$ where $P_{ion} = D_{ion}/d$ is the permeability coefficient. **Step 3 — Apply Zero-Net-Current Condition** At steady state, the membrane cannot accumulate net charge, so: $$ I_{Na} + I_{K} + I_{Cl} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Substitute and Solve for $V_m$** Solving the resulting algebraic expression for $V_m$: $$ \boxed{V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}} $$ This is the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pearl**: Notice that $Cl^-$ appears **inverted** in the numerator/denominator (compared to cations). This is because $Cl^-$ has *negative valence* — the algebra inverts its concentration terms automatically. Always double-check this sign inversion in exam answers. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples #### **Example 1 — Calculate $E_K$ at 37 °C** Given: $[K^+]_{in} = 140\ \text{mM}$, $[K^+]_{out} = 5\ \text{mM}$. $$ E_K = 61.5 \log_{10}\dfrac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}} $$ $$ E_K = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{5}{140}\right) $$ $$ E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.03571) $$ $$ E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.4472) $$ $$ \boxed{E_K \approx -89.0\ \text{mV}} $$ #### **Example 2 — Calculate $E_{Na}$ at 37 °C** Given: $[Na^+]_{in} = 10\ \text{mM}$, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145\ \text{mM}$. $$ E_{Na} = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{145}{10}\right) $$ $$ E_{Na} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(14.5) $$ $$ E_{Na} = 61.5 \times 1.1614 $$ $$ \boxed{E_{Na} \approx +71.4\ \text{mV}} $$ #### **Example 3 — Calculate $V_m$ Using the Goldman Equation** Assume relative permeabilities: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$. Using the concentrations from Section 2.1: $$ V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\dfrac{(1)(5) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(120)} $$ **Numerator**: $$ 5 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 12.6 $$ **Denominator**: $$ 140 + 0.4 + 54 = 194.4 $$ $$ V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{12.6}{194.4}\right) $$ $$ V_m = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.0648) $$ $$ V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.188) $$ $$ \boxed{V_m \approx -73.1\ \text{mV}} $$ This matches the experimentally observed resting potential of a typical mammalian neuron ($-70\ \text{mV}$). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Equation to Use?" | Situation in Exam | Use This Equation | | :--- | :--- | | Only **one** ion is permeable | Nernst Equation | | $V_m$ of a *real* neuron with multiple permeabilities | Goldman (GHK) Equation | | Computing single-ion flux | Nernst-Planck | | Determining charge stored / time-course of $V_m$ change | $Q = C_m V_m$, $\tau = R_m C_m$ | | $V_m$ value at instant all channels close (capacitor only) | Held fixed (no current) | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Membrane Potential Architecture (Ion Players + Equation Relationships) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Na K ATPase Pump
3 Na out 2 K in per ATP] --> B[Concentration Gradients
High K inside High Na outside] B --> C{Nernst Equation
Eion RT zF ln out in} B --> D{Goldman GHK Equation
Vm RT F ln weighted ratio} C --> E[Equilibrium Potentials
E K around minus 90 mV
E Na around plus 60 mV] D --> F[Resting Membrane Potential
Vm around minus 70 mV] G[Leak K Channels
Always open] --> F H[Voltage Gated Na Channels
Closed at rest] -.-> F I[Leak Cl Channels
Minor contribution] --> F E --> F F --> J[Threshold minus 55 mV
Triggers Action Potential] J --> K[Action Potential Peak
plus 30 to plus 40 mV] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of $V_m$ Generation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ENERGY[Energy Source Layer] ATP[ATP Hydrolysis] end subgraph PUMP[Pump Layer Electrogenic] NAATP[Na K ATPase
3 Na out 2 K in] end subgraph GRADIENT[Ion Gradient Layer] KGRAD[K High inside 140 mM
K Low outside 5 mM] NAGRAD[Na Low inside 10 mM
Na High outside 145 mM] CLGRAD[Cl Low inside 4 mM
Cl High outside 120 mM] end subgraph CHANNELS[Ion Channel Layer Selective Permeability] KCH[Leak K Channels
Permeability PK 1 0] NACH[Voltage Gated Na
Permeability PNa 0 04] CLCH[Leak Cl Channels
Permeability PCl 0 45] end subgraph COMPUTE[Mathematical Layer] NERNST[Nernst Equation
Per ion equilibrium] GHK[Goldman GHK Equation
Weighted Vm] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer Membrane Potential] VM[Resting Vm
minus 70 mV] THRESH[Threshold
minus 55 mV] AP[Action Potential
plus 30 mV] end ATP --> NAATP NAATP --> KGRAD NAATP --> NAGRAD NAATP --> CLGRAD KGRAD --> KCH NAGRAD --> NACH CLGRAD --> CLCH KCH --> NERNST NACH --> NERNST CLCH --> NERNST KCH --> GHK NACH --> GHK CLCH --> GHK NERNST --> GHK GHK --> VM VM --> THRESH THRESH --> AP ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Nernst Permeability Effect on $V_m$ (Slider Concept) ```mermaid flowchart LR Kdom[K Dominant Permeability
P K 1 0] --> KVM[Vm pulls toward E K
minus 90 mV] Nadom[Na Dominant Permeability
P Na 1 0] --> NAVM[Vm pulls toward E Na
plus 60 mV] Cldom[Cl Dominant Permeability
P Cl 1 0] --> CLVM[Vm pulls toward E Cl
minus 90 mV] Mix[Mixed Permeability
Real neuron case] --> REST[Vm at intermediate value
minus 70 mV] Kdom --> Mix Nadom --> Mix Cldom --> Mix ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams**: These schematics map the *causal chain* of $V_m$ generation — from ATP-driven pumping → gradients → channel selectivity → mathematical blend (Goldman) → observed resting potential. Each block corresponds directly to a section of the KTU 2024 syllabus. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** Define the Nernst equation and explain its significance in determining the equilibrium potential of an ion across a biological membrane. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Nernst equation gives the membrane voltage at which there is **no net flow** of a particular ion across a permeable membrane. It is stated as: $$ E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} $$ where $R$ is the gas constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, $z$ is ion valence, and $F$ is Faraday's constant. **Significance**: 1. It provides the **equilibrium potential** for a *single* ion species (e.g., $E_K \approx -90\ \text{mV}$, $E_{Na} \approx +60\ \text{mV}$). 2. It quantifies the contribution of an ion's *concentration gradient* to the membrane potential. 3. It is the foundational relation that, when combined for multiple ions, yields the more comprehensive **Goldman equation**. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Distinguish between *equilibrium potential* and *resting membrane potential*. Why does the resting potential lie closer to $E_K$ than to $E_{Na}$? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Equilibrium Potential ($E_{ion}$) | Resting Membrane Potential ($V_m$) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Definition | $V_m$ at which net flux of *one* ion = 0 | Actual $V_m$ of a neuron at rest | | Applies to | Single ion | All permeant ions simultaneously | | Equation | Nernst | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz | | Value | $E_K \approx -90\ \text{mV}$; $E_{Na} \approx +60\ \text{mV}$ | $\approx -70\ \text{mV}$ | **Why closer to $E_K$?** Because at rest the neuronal membrane is **~25 times more permeable to $K^+$** than to $Na^+$ (more leak $K^+$ channels are open). Since $V_m$ is a *weighted average* of ionic equilibrium potentials (with weights = permeabilities, per the Goldman equation), the dominant $P_K$ pulls $V_m$ toward $-90\ \text{mV}$ rather than toward $+60\ \text{mV}$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- **QUESTION A. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Module 2]`** **(a)** Derive the Nernst equation starting from the principle of electrochemical equilibrium. Clearly state each thermodynamic step. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A neuron has the following ionic concentrations: * $[K^+]_{in} = 140\ \text{mM}$, $[K^+]_{out} = 5\ \text{mM}$ * $[Na^+]_{in} = 12\ \text{mM}$, $[Na^+]_{out} = 144\ \text{mM}$ Calculate $E_K$ and $E_{Na}$ at body temperature ($37^\circ\text{C}$) using the log₁₀ form of the Nernst equation. Comment on which ion is closer to its equilibrium at a typical resting $V_m$ of $-70\ \text{mV}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation:** *Step 1 — Free Energy of Chemical Diffusion:* When $1$ mole of ions moves from outside to inside against a concentration gradient, the free energy change is: $$ \Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}} $$ *Step 2 — Electrical Work Done:* Moving $1$ mole of ions of valence $z$ across a potential difference $V_m$ requires: $$ W_{elec} = z F V_m $$ *Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition:* At equilibrium, total free energy change = 0: $$ RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}} + z F V_m = 0 $$ *Step 4 — Solving for $V_m$:* Rearranging gives: $$ \boxed{E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}} $$ Converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ multiplies by $2.303$, giving the familiar form: $$ E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} $$ At $37^\circ\text{C}$, $2.303\,RT/F = 61.5\ \text{mV}$, hence: $$ \boxed{E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\ \text{mV}} $$ **Part (b) — Calculation:** For $K^+$ ($z = +1$): $$ E_K = \dfrac{61.5}{+1}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{5}{140}\right) = 61.5 \times (-1.4472) \approx -89.0\ \text{mV} $$ For $Na^+$ ($z = +1$): $$ E_{Na} = \dfrac{61.5}{+1}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{144}{12}\right) = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(12) \approx 61.5 \times 1.0792 \approx +66.4\ \text{mV} $$ **Comment on closeness to $V_m = -70\ \text{mV}$:** * Driving force on $K^+$ at rest $= V_m - E_K = -70 - (-89) = +19\ \text{mV}$. (A small driving force → low net $K^+$ flux.) * Driving force on $Na^+$ at rest $= V_m - E_{Na} = -70 - (+66.4) = -136.4\ \text{mV}$. (A *very large* inward driving force, but limited by low permeability.) **Conclusion**: The resting potential is much closer to $E_K$ ($-89\ \text{mV}$) than to $E_{Na}$ ($+66.4\ \text{mV}$). This occurs because the membrane is dominantly permeable to $K^+$, so $V_m$ settles *near* $E_K$ — not because the ionic gradient itself dictates the value. --- **QUESTION B. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 2]`** **(a)** State the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation. Explain each term. Why is the contribution of $Cl^-$ listed with *inverted* concentration terms compared to $K^+$ and $Na^+$? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Using the data below and the GHK equation, calculate the resting membrane potential of a neuron at $37^\circ\text{C}$. Relative permeabilities: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.05 : 0.4$. **(7 Marks)** | Ion | Inside $[\text{mM}]$ | Outside $[\text{mM}]$ | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | $K^+$ | 150 | 6 | | $Na^+$ | 15 | 150 | | $Cl^-$ | 6 | 125 | **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — GHK Equation:** $$ V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}} $$ *Term meanings*: * $R$, $T$, $F$: thermodynamic constants. * $P_{ion}$: permeability coefficient (cm/s) — weight for each ion's contribution. * $[ion]$: molar concentrations inside/outside. **Why $Cl^-$ is inverted**: Because $Cl^-$ carries a *negative* charge ($z = -1$). Mathematically, in the GHK derivation, the algebra naturally inverts anion concentrations. Physically, this reflects the fact that a higher $[Cl^-]_{out}$ *depolarizes* the cell from $Cl^-$'s perspective (driving force is inward), but the *mathematical sign* requires the inversion for the GHK formula to yield correct $V_m$ in mV. **Part (b) — Calculation:** Compute numerator and denominator (using the log₁₀ form for direct mV): **Numerator**: $$ P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in} $$ $$ = (1)(6) + (0.05)(150) + (0.4)(6) = 6 + 7.5 + 2.4 = 15.9 $$ **Denominator**: $$ P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out} $$ $$ = (1)(150) + (0.05)(15) + (0.4)(125) = 150 + 0.75 + 50 = 200.75 $$ **Apply GHK**: $$ V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{15.9}{200.75}\right) $$ $$ V_m = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.0792) $$ $$ V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.1013) $$ $$ \boxed{V_m \approx -67.7\ \text{mV}} $$ This is consistent with the physiologically observed resting membrane potential of $\approx -70\ \text{mV}$ for typical mammalian neurons. The slight discrepancy arises from the omission of $Ca^{2+}$ permeability and active pump electrogenicity. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the inversion of $[Cl^-]$ terms** in the Goldman equation (most common error — costs 2–3 marks). > 2. **Using $z = +1$ for anions** in the Nernst equation — must use the *actual* valence, which is negative for $Cl^-$. > 3. **Mixing up $\ln$ and $\log_{10}$** — forgetting the $2.303$ factor results in a $\sim 2.3\times$ error in $V_m$. > 4. **Forgetting the temperature condition** — the $61.5\ \text{mV}$ factor is valid *only* at $37^\circ\text{C}$; at room temperature ($25^\circ\text{C}$) it is $59.1\ \text{mV}$. > 5. **Confusing "driving force" with "equilibrium potential"** — driving force $= V_m - E_{ion}$, not $E_{ion}$ itself. > 6. **Units**: Always state the final answer in **mV** (or convert explicitly from Volts by $\times 1000$). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist * **Membrane potential** is the voltage difference across the plasma membrane ($-70\ \text{mV}$ at rest, inside negative). * **Three pillars**: ion gradients, selective permeability, active $Na^+/K^+$ ATPase pumping. * **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase**: 3 $Na^+$ out, 2 $K^+$ in per ATP; electrogenic (net +1 charge exported). * **Nernst Equation**: gives equilibrium potential of *one* ion; uses $\dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln$ or $\dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}$ at $37^\circ\text{C}$. * **Goldman Equation**: weighted average $V_m$ across *multiple* permeant ions; weights = permeabilities. * **$Cl^-$ term is inverted** in the GHK equation (because $z = -1$). * **Driving force** on an ion $= V_m - E_{ion}$; small driving force → low flux. * **Resting $V_m$ is close to $E_K$** because $P_K \gg P_{Na}$ at rest (~25:1). * **$Ca^{2+}$ contributes minimally** to resting $V_m$ (very low $P_{Ca}$ at rest) but dominates *excitation-secretion coupling*. * **Membrane time constant** $\tau = R_m C_m \approx 10\ \text{ms}$ governs how fast $V_m$ can change. * **Remember the constants**: $R = 8.314\ \text{J/mol·K}$, $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$, $T = 310\ \text{K}$, factor $= 61.5\ \text{mV}$ at $37^\circ\text{C}$. * **Internal concentration of $K^+$** is high (~140 mM); **internal $Na^+$** is low (~10 mM) — these gradients are *maintained by pumping*, not by the channels. * **Permeability, not concentration, determines which ion's $E_{ion}$ dominates** $V_m$.

topics.title: Local Signaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT752 - NEUROBIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Local Signaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧠 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Signaling in Neurobiology** refers to the restricted, short-range intercellular communication mechanisms employed by neurons and glial cells to transmit information over microscopic distances (typically **< 100 μm**) without requiring action potential propagation along long axonal pathways. It encompasses **paracrine transmission, autocrine regulation, synaptic neurotransmission, electrical coupling via gap junctions, and volume transmission** within confined neuropil territories.

In the context of KTU Module 2, local signaling forms the foundational layer of neuronal information transfer that operates *before*, *during*, and *after* classical action potential propagation. The defining feature is **spatial restriction** — the signal does not travel globally through the bloodstream (endocrine) but acts on immediately adjacent cells within a discrete microenvironment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Neighborhood Telegraph

Imagine a large apartment complex (the brain):

- **Synaptic signaling** is like a precise, wired intercom between two adjacent apartments — the message goes only to one designated listener, instantly.
- **Paracrine signaling** is like shouting from one balcony — your neighbors on either side may hear you, but the people on the top floor cannot.
- **Autocrine signaling** is like talking to yourself in the mirror — the message loops back to influence your own behavior.
- **Gap junction signaling** is like an open window between two apartments — anything said is heard directly, with zero delay.
- **Volume transmission** is like a fragrance that drifts through shared hallways — it reaches everyone in the corridor but loses intensity with distance.

## 1.3 Classification of Local Signaling Modes

| Mode | Range | Speed | Carrier Molecule | Example in Nervous System |
|------|-------|-------|------------------|---------------------------|
| **Synaptic (Wired)** | **~20-50 nm** cleft | **< 1 ms** | Classical neurotransmitters (glutamate, GABA, ACh) | Neuromuscular junction |
| **Paracrine** | **< 100 μm** | **ms – s** | Neuropeptides, ATP, growth factors | Astrocyte-to-neuron gliotransmission |
| **Autocrine** | **Self** | **s – min** | BDNF, cytokines | Neuronal self-modulation of firing |
| **Gap Junction (Electrical)** | **Direct cytoplasm** | **Sub-ms** | Ions (current flow) | Hippocampal interneuron networks |
| **Volume Transmission** | **100 μm – 1 mm** | **100s of ms** | Monoamines, NO, endocannabinoids | Dopamine release in striatum |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants in Local Signaling

> [!NOTE]
> - **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/mol
> - **Gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
> - **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96485$ C/mol
> - **Body temperature (physiological):** $T = 310$ K (i.e., **37 °C**)
> - **Synaptic cleft width:** $\approx 20-40$ nm
> - **Diffusion coefficient of small neurotransmitters:** $D \approx 10^{-6}$ cm²/s

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Local Signaling

Local signaling in neurons follows a **four-stage operational loop** that recurs in every neurobiological textbook:

1. **Synthesis & Vesicular Loading** — Neurotransmitters (NTs) or neuropeptides are packaged into synaptic vesicles by specialized transporters (e.g., VMAT for monoamines, VGluT for glutamate).
2. **Triggered Release** — An action potential invades the presynaptic terminal, depolarizing it and opening voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels. The resulting Ca²⁺ influx triggers vesicle fusion via the SNARE complex.
3. **Local Diffusion Across the Cleft** — NTs traverse the **20–40 nm synaptic cleft** in microseconds, binding to postsynaptic receptors confined to the density.
4. **Receptor-Mediated Response** — Ionotropic receptors (fast) or metabotropic receptors (slow) generate local graded potentials, modulating the postsynaptic neuron's excitability.

## 2.2 Detailed Sub-Modalities

### 2.2.1 Paracrine Signaling in the CNS
- **Mechanism:** Cells release signaling molecules (e.g., ATP, adenosine, BDNF, NO) into the extracellular fluid.
- **Reach:** Acts on multiple neighboring cells within a 20–100 μm radius.
- **Example:** Astrocytes releasing **D-serine** and **glutamate** to modulate nearby NMDA receptors (the tripartite synapse).

### 2.2.2 Autocrine Signaling
- **Mechanism:** A neuron releases a signal that binds to receptors on its own membrane.
- **Example:** A dopaminergic neuron releasing dopamine that activates D₂ autoreceptors on its own soma/terminals, providing **negative feedback inhibition** of further release.

### 2.2.3 Synaptic (Wired) Transmission
- **Most precise** form of local signaling.
- **One-to-one** stoichiometry (one presynaptic terminal → one postsynaptic density).
- **Two receptor classes:**
  - *Ionotropic* (ligand-gated ion channels) — fast, millisecond kinetics.
  - *Metabotropic* (GPCRs) — slow, second-messenger mediated, seconds to minutes.

### 2.2.4 Gap Junctions (Electrical Synapses)
- Composed of **connexin proteins** (Cx36 in neurons) forming hexameric **connexons**.
- Allow direct ionic current flow between coupled cells.
- Bidirectional; enable **synchronization** of neuronal ensembles (e.g., hippocampal interneurons generating gamma oscillations).

### 2.2.5 Volume Transmission
- **Diffuse** extrasynaptic signaling through the extracellular space.
- Monoamines (dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine) often escape the synaptic cleft and act on distant receptors.
- Critical in **neuromodulation** rather than point-to-point information transfer.

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why vesicles?** Vesicles protect NTs from cytoplasmic enzymes and concentrate them for **quantal release** (each vesicle = one quantum).
- **Why Ca²⁺?** Ca²⁺ is the universal **excitation-secretion coupling** ion — its intracellular concentration is kept ~10,000× lower than extracellular, providing a steep electrochemical gradient for rapid, large influx upon channel opening.
- **Why local?** Local signaling is **energy-efficient** and **temporally precise** — long-distance endocrine signaling would be too slow for sensory-motor integration.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable under KTU ESE for PEBBT752. Memorize the symbols, units, and physiological meaning.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $J = -D \, \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's First Law of Diffusion (flux of NT) | mol/(cm²·s) |
| 2 | $t_{\text{diff}} = \dfrac{x^2}{2D}$ | Time for NT to diffuse distance $x$ | seconds |
| 3 | $E_{\text{ion}} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$ | Nernst Equation (equilibrium potential) | volts |
| 4 | $E_{\text{ion}} \approx \dfrac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$ | Nernst at 37 °C (KTU simplified form) | mV |
| 5 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation | volts |
| 6 | $I_{\text{syn}} = g_{\text{syn}} \, (V_m - E_{\text{syn}})$ | Synaptic current (driving force × conductance) | amperes |
| 7 | $\tau_m = R_m \, C_m$ | Membrane time constant | seconds |
| 8 | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | Length constant (passive cable) | cm |
| 9 | $g_{\text{syn}}(t) = \bar{g} \, \left( e^{-t/\tau_{\text{decay}}} - e^{-t/\tau_{\text{rise}}} \right)$ | Synaptic conductance waveform | siemens |
| 10 | $[\text{Ca}^{2+}]_i \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Vesicle Fusion}$ | Excitation-secretion coupling (qualitative) | qualitative |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs):** Local field potentials (LFPs) recorded via Utah arrays are generated by summed local synaptic currents — understanding this is critical for decoding motor intent.
- **Pharmacology/Drug Design:** Local signaling principles guide design of **selective reuptake inhibitors** (SSRIs), **receptor antagonists**, and **gap junction blockers** (mefloquine, carbenoxolone).
- **Neuroprosthetics:** Engineers exploit graded local signaling in retinal implants where electrical stimulation of bipolar cells mimics natural local transmission.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Time for Neurotransmitter to Cross the Synaptic Cleft

**Statement:** Show that a glutamate molecule takes approximately **5–10 microseconds** to diffuse across a 25 nm synaptic cleft.

Starting from Fick's Second Law in one dimension:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

For a point source releasing $N$ molecules at $x=0, t=0$, the solution is the Gaussian profile:

$$C(x,t) = \frac{N}{\sqrt{4 \pi D t}} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)$$

The **mean-square displacement** of a diffusing particle is:

$$\langle x^2 \rangle = 2 D t$$

Solving for the diffusion time $t$ to traverse a distance $x$:

$$t_{\text{diff}} = \frac{x^2}{2D}$$

**Substitute the synaptic parameters** (numerical evaluation):

- Cleft width: $x = 25 \text{ nm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ cm
- Diffusion coefficient (glutamate in water): $D = 7.0 \times 10^{-6}$ cm²/s

$$t_{\text{diff}} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-6})^2}{2 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 1:** Square the numerator:

$$(2.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-12} \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Multiply denominator:

$$2 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-6} = 1.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Divide:

$$t_{\text{diff}} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-12}}{1.4 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.46 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \approx 0.45 \, \mu s$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $t_{\text{diff}} \approx 0.5$ μs. This explains the **sub-millisecond synaptic delay** observed at chemical synapses — diffusion is not the rate-limiting step; **vesicle fusion and receptor gating** dominate.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nernst Equation from Electrochemical Equilibrium

**Statement:** Derive the equilibrium potential for a monovalent ion (e.g., K⁺) using thermodynamic equilibrium of electrochemical potential.

The electrochemical potential of an ion on side 1 vs side 2 must be equal at equilibrium:

$$\mu_{\text{ion,1}} = \mu_{\text{ion,2}}$$

Expanding:

$$\mu^0 + RT \ln[\text{ion}]_1 + z F V_1 = \mu^0 + RT \ln[\text{ion}]_2 + z F V_2$$

Cancel $\mu^0$:

$$RT \ln[\text{ion}]_1 + z F V_1 = RT \ln[\text{ion}]_2 + z F V_2$$

Rearrange:

$$z F (V_2 - V_1) = RT \ln\frac{[\text{ion}]_1}{[\text{ion}]_2}$$

Define the equilibrium potential $E_{\text{ion}} = V_1 - V_2$ (inside relative to outside):

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$$

**Converting to base-10 logarithm at 37 °C** (using $2.303 \, RT/F = 61.5$ mV at 310 K):

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10}\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$$

**Numerical check for K⁺** with typical neuronal values:

- $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM, $z = +1$

$$E_K = 61 \log_{10}\frac{5}{140} = 61 \log_{10}(0.0357)$$

$$= 61 \times (-1.447) = -88.3 \text{ mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $E_K \approx -88$ mV, matching the experimentally measured K⁺ equilibrium potential in mammalian neurons.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Synaptic Current Driving Postsynaptic Potential

**Statement:** Express the postsynaptic membrane potential change due to a single quantal release.

The synaptic current is:

$$I_{\text{syn}}(t) = g_{\text{syn}}(t) \, [V_m(t) - E_{\text{syn}}]$$

For a **small** conductance change $\Delta g$ and assuming $V_m \approx V_{\text{rest}}$ (linear approximation):

$$I_{\text{syn}}(t) \approx \Delta g(t) \, [V_{\text{rest}} - E_{\text{syn}}]$$

The postsynaptic potential change follows:

$$\Delta V_m(t) = \frac{1}{C_m} \int_0^t I_{\text{syn}}(t') \, e^{-(t-t')/\tau_m} \, dt'$$

For instantaneous conductance change $\Delta g = \bar{g}$ at $t=0$:

$$\Delta V_m(t) = \frac{\bar{g} \cdot (E_{\text{syn}} - V_{\text{rest}})}{C_m} \, \tau_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Peak EPSP amplitude** (at $t \to \infty$):

$$\Delta V_{\text{peak}} = \frac{\bar{g} \cdot (E_{\text{syn}} - V_{\text{rest}}) \cdot \tau_m}{C_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The peak postsynaptic potential is **proportional** to (i) synaptic conductance $\bar{g}$, (ii) driving force $(E_{\text{syn}} - V_{\text{rest}})$, and (iii) membrane time constant $\tau_m$, and **inversely proportional** to membrane capacitance $C_m$. This is the cornerstone of synaptic integration.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Quantitative Comparison of Local Signaling Modes

| Parameter | Synaptic | Paracrine | Gap Junction | Volume |
|-----------|----------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| Spatial range | 20-50 nm | 10-100 μm | 0 nm (direct) | 100 μm - 1 mm |
| Temporal delay | < 1 ms | 1-100 ms | < 0.1 ms | 10-1000 ms |
| Signaling molecule | Glutamate, GABA | ATP, BDNF, NO | Inions (current) | Dopamine, 5-HT |
| Receptor type | Ionotropic / GPCR | GPCR, RTK | Connexon channels | GPCR |
| Energy cost (ATP) | High (vesicle cycle) | Medium | Low | Low |
| Directionality | Unidirectional | Multidirectional | Bidirectional | Multidirectional |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Taxonomy of Local Signaling in the Nervous System

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Sequence of Local Synaptic Transmission (Excitation-Secretion Coupling)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PreT as Presynaptic Terminal
    participant CaCh as Voltage Gated Ca2+ Channel
    participant Ves as Synaptic Vesicle
    participant Cleft as Synaptic Cleft 20 to 40 nm
    participant PostR as Postsynaptic Receptor
    participant PostM as Postsynaptic Membrane
    
    PreT->>PreT: Action Potential Arrival Depolarization
    PreT->>CaCh: Membrane Voltage Rises
    CaCh->>PreT: Ca2+ Influx Down Gradient
    PreT->>Ves: Ca2+ Triggers SNARE Complex Docking
    Ves->>Cleft: Vesicle Fusion Exocytosis NT Release
    Cleft->>PostR: NT Diffuses Across Cleft
    PostR->>PostM: Receptor Activation
    PostM->>PostM: Local Graded Potential EPSP or IPSP
    PostR->>Cleft: NT Clearance by Reuptake or Degradation
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tripartite Synapse

```mermaid

        AR["Astrocyte Receptors"]
    end
    
    AP1 --> AX
    AX --> RYA
    VS --> RYA
    RYA --> DIFF

    DIFF --> MR

    MR --> SP

    AR --> GS
    
    style PRESYNAPTIC_NEURON fill:#dbeafe

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between paracrine and autocrine signaling in the nervous system with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Paracrine | Autocrine |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Target cell | **Neighboring cells** | **The secreting cell itself** |
| Range | 10–100 μm | Self (receptor on same cell) |
| Example in CNS | Astrocyte release of **D-serine** onto nearby neurons | Dopaminergic neuron releasing dopamine onto its own **D2 autoreceptor** |

> **[Mark Distribution: 1.5 marks for table-style differentiation + 1.5 marks for examples]**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is volume transmission? Mention its physiological significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Volume transmission is a form of local signaling where neurotransmitters (commonly monoamines such as dopamine, serotonin) escape the synaptic cleft and diffuse through the extracellular space to act on extrasynaptic receptors over distances of **100 μm to 1 mm**.

**Physiological significance (any two for 1.5 marks):**
1. Enables **diffuse neuromodulation** of large brain regions simultaneously.
2. Provides a **slower temporal scale** (hundreds of milliseconds) complementary to fast synaptic transmission.
3. Forms the basis of action of many **psychiatric drugs** (SSRIs, antipsychotics).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **commonly lose 2-3 marks** for writing the Nernst equation without specifying the **sign convention** (inside relative to outside).
> - Avoid **mixing up** the Goldman equation variables — remember: permeabilities multiply **OUTSIDE concentrations for cations and INSIDE for anions**.
> - Always mention **temperature (37 °C / 310 K)** when using the 61 mV Nernst constant, otherwise the derivation is incomplete (lose 1 mark).
> - For synaptic transmission sub-questions, **draw a labeled diagram** (2 marks reserved for diagram quality).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Set B]`

**a) With a neat labeled diagram, explain the sequence of events involved in chemical synaptic transmission. Discuss the role of Ca²⁺ as the key coupling ion. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Diagram (2 marks):** Draw a presynaptic terminal with vesicles, voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels, synaptic cleft (label 20–40 nm), postsynaptic density with receptors, and reuptake transporters.

**(ii) Step-by-step narrative (5 marks):**

1. **Action potential invasion** — The AP traveling down the axon reaches the presynaptic terminal, depolarizing the membrane.
2. **Opening of voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels** — Depolarization activates Cav2.1 (P/Q-type) channels.
3. **Ca²⁺ influx** — Extracellular Ca²⁺ (~2 mM) flows down its steep electrochemical gradient into the terminal (~100 nM resting).
4. **Ca²⁺ binds synaptotagmin** — This is the Ca²⁺ sensor on synaptic vesicles, triggering the SNARE-mediated fusion of vesicles with the presynaptic membrane.
5. **Exocytosis** — Vesicles release neurotransmitter (e.g., glutamate) into the synaptic cleft.
6. **Diffusion & receptor binding** — NT crosses the cleft (~0.5 μs) and binds postsynaptic receptors.
7. **Postsynaptic response** — Generates EPSP/IPSP (local graded potential).
8. **Termination** — NT cleared by reuptake, enzymatic degradation, or diffusion.

**Role of Ca²⁺ (integrated above):** Ca²⁺ is the **excitation-secretion coupler** — it converts an electrical signal (AP) into a chemical signal (NT release). Its steep inward gradient provides both **speed and amplification** (one channel opening can release multiple vesicles).

---

**b) Derive the Nernst equation for a monovalent cation and calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37 °C given [K⁺]ₒ = 4 mM and [K⁺]ᵢ = 140 mM. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** At electrochemical equilibrium, the chemical potential gradient balances the electrical gradient:

$$\mu_{\text{out}} + zFV_{\text{out}} = \mu_{\text{in}} + zFV_{\text{in}}$$

Expanding the chemical potential $\mu = \mu^0 + RT \ln[\text{ion}]$:

$$\mu^0 + RT \ln[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}} + zFV_{\text{out}} = \mu^0 + RT \ln[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}} + zFV_{\text{in}}$$

Cancel $\mu^0$ and rearrange:

$$zF(V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}) = RT \ln\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$$

Define $E_{\text{ion}} = V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}$:

$$\boxed{E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}}$$

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):** Converting to base-10 logarithm at 37 °C (310 K):

$$E_K = \frac{61 \text{ mV}}{+1} \log_{10}\frac{4}{140}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{4}{140} = 0.02857$$

**Step 2:** Take log₁₀:

$$\log_{10}(0.02857) = -1.544$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by 61 mV:

$$E_K = 61 \times (-1.544) = -94.2 \text{ mV}$$

> **[Result: $E_K \approx -94$ mV]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]`

**a) Explain the structure and function of gap junctions in the nervous system. How do they differ from chemical synapses? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Structure (2 marks):**
- Gap junctions are formed by **connexin proteins** (Cx36 predominant in neurons).
- Six connexins form a **connexon (hemichannel)**.
- Two connexons from adjacent cells dock to form an intercellular **channel** (~1.2 nm pore).
- Multiple channels cluster into a **plaque** (up to thousands of channels).

**Function (2 marks):**
- Allow direct **ionic current flow** between coupled cells.
- Mediate **synchronization** of neuronal firing (e.g., hippocampal interneurons generating 30–80 Hz gamma oscillations).
- Enable **bidirectional** transfer of small metabolites (IP₃, cAMP) up to ~1 kDa.

**Comparison with chemical synapses (3 marks):**

| Feature | Gap Junction | Chemical Synapse |
|---------|--------------|------------------|
| Speed | Sub-millisecond | ~0.5–1 ms delay |
| Direction | Bidirectional | Unidirectional |
| Signal | Ionic current | Neurotransmitter |
| Plasticity | Limited (modulated by pH, Ca²⁺) | High (LTP, LTD) |
| Energy cost | Low | High (vesicle cycle) |

---

**b) Describe the tripartite synapse. How does astrocyte-neuron local signaling modulate synaptic transmission? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **tripartite synapse** is a functional unit consisting of:
1. The **presynaptic neuron terminal**.
2. The **postsynaptic neuron**.
3. The **astrocyte** (a type of glial cell) ensheathing the synapse.

**Astrocyte functions (3 marks):**
- **Glutamate uptake:** EAAT1/EAAT2 transporters on astrocytes clear ~90% of synaptic glutamate, terminating its action.
- **Gliotransmitter release:** Astrocytes release **D-serine** (a co-agonist at NMDA receptors), **glutamate**, **ATP/adenosine**, modulating synaptic strength.
- **K⁺ buffering:** They take up excess extracellular K⁺ after neural activity, maintaining ion homeostasis.
- **Ca²⁺ signaling:** Astrocytes exhibit Ca²⁺ waves that propagate to neighboring synapses.

**Modulation of synaptic transmission (2 marks):**
- **D-serine** released by astrocytes is essential for NMDA receptor activation (which requires both glutamate AND a co-agonist).
- **Adenosine** from ATP breakdown activates A1 receptors, presynaptically inhibiting NT release.
- This forms a **feedback loop** where local neuronal activity dynamically alters astrocytic state, which in turn modulates future synaptic efficacy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧠 Rapid-Revision Checklist for Local Signaling:**

- ⭐ **Local signaling** = intercellular communication over < 100 μm; does **not** require long axonal propagation or bloodstream transport.
- ⭐ **Five major modes:** Synaptic (wired), Paracrine, Autocrine, Gap junction (electrical), Volume transmission.
- ⭐ **Synaptic cleft width:** **20–40 nm** — diffusion across it takes **< 1 μs** (calculated via $t = x^2/2D$).
- ⭐ **Excitation-secretion coupling** is mediated by **Ca²⁺** entering through voltage-gated channels.
- ⭐ **Nernst equation** (at 37 °C): $E = 61 \text{ mV}/z \times \log_{10}([\text{out}]/[\text{in}])$.
- ⭐ **Synaptic current:** $I_{\text{syn}} = g_{\text{syn}} (V_m - E_{\text{syn}})$.
- ⭐ **Gap junctions** use **connexins** (Cx36 in neurons), are **bidirectional**, and mediate sub-millisecond electrical coupling.
- ⭐ **Tripartite synapse** = pre + post + astrocyte; astrocytes release **D-serine** to gate NMDA receptors.
- ⭐ **Autoreceptors** (e.g., D2) provide **negative feedback** — a key example of autocrine signaling.
- ⭐ **Volume transmission** is **slow but spatially broad** — critical for neuromodulation by monoamines.
- ⭐ **Ionotropic** = fast (ms), **Metabotropic** = slow (s-min) — always specify receptor class when discussing local signaling kinetics.
- ⭐ **Energy hierarchy:** Gap junction < Volume < Paracrine < Autocrine < Synaptic wired.
- ⭐ Always mention **temperature (310 K / 37 °C)** in Nernst-related derivations to earn full marks.
- ⭐ **Clinical correlate:** Many neurological drugs (SSRIs, antipsychotics) act by modulating local synaptic or volume transmission.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Action Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT752 - NEUROBIOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Action Potential # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) An **Action Potential (AP)** is a rapid, transient, all-or-none electrical signal generated by an excitable cell (principally a neuron or muscle fiber) in which the membrane potential undergoes a stereotyped, regenerative reversal of polarity, propagating without decrement along the length of the axon to transmit information over long distances. In the language of the **Hodgkin–Huxley formalism (1952)** — the foundation adopted in the KTU 2024 Neurobiology syllabus — the action potential is described as a self-propagating wave of **Na⁺ and K⁺ ionic conductance changes** across a lipid bilayer governed by **voltage-gated ion channels**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEBBT752, Module 2):** > The action potential is a **regenerative depolarization** that occurs only when the membrane potential reaches a critical **threshold (typically ≈ −55 mV)**. Below this threshold, sub-threshold graded potentials decay; above it, the AP fires with fixed amplitude regardless of stimulus strength (All-or-None Principle). Mathematically, the resting membrane potential is defined as: $$V_m(t) = \frac{g_{Na}(t)\,E_{Na} + g_K(t)\,E_K + g_L\,E_L}{g_{Na}(t) + g_K(t) + g_L}$$ where $g_{Na}(t)$ and $g_K(t)$ are the **time- and voltage-dependent conductances** of sodium and potassium channels respectively, $g_L$ is the constant leak conductance, and $E_{Na}$ and $E_K$ are the Nernst reversal potentials for Na⁺ and K⁺. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Dominos on a Hill** > Imagine a long row of dominoes standing on a slope. The slope is the **resting membrane potential** (the cell is already polarized, "ready to roll"). A single flick of a finger — too weak — only nudges the first domino a little, and the wave dies out. That is a **sub-threshold graded potential**. But if you push the first domino **hard enough** (crossing the threshold), it tumbles into the second, the second into the third, and so on. The cascade is self-sustaining and travels the entire row at fixed speed. That is an **action potential** — a regenerative, all-or-none wave. A second analogy is the **electrical fuse**: once the current exceeds a certain critical value, the fuse blows completely and definitively, regardless of how much extra current is applied. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Must Memorize) | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Resting Potential ($V_m$)** | **−70 mV** | Baseline polarized state | | **Threshold ($V_{th}$)** | **≈ −55 mV** | Trigger point for AP | | **Peak Depolarization** | **≈ +30 to +40 mV** | Maximum inside-positive | | **Hyperpolarization (after-potential)** | **≈ −80 mV** | Undershoot below resting | | **AP Duration** | **1 – 2 ms** (neuron) | Speed of one full cycle | | **AP Amplitude** | **~100–110 mV** | Total swing from peak to resting | | **Conduction Velocity** | **1 – 120 m/s** | Varies with axon diameter & myelination | | **Nernst Potential $E_{Na}$** | **+60 mV** | Na⁺ equilibrium | | **Nernst Potential $E_K$** | **−90 mV** | K⁺ equilibrium | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Phases of an Action Potential — Structured Logic ### **Phase 0 — Resting State** - **Voltage:** $V_m = -70$ mV. - **Why:** The Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in) maintains ionic gradients; leak channels allow more K⁺ efflux than Na⁺ influx. - **How:** $g_K \gg g_{Na}$, so $V_m$ sits near $E_K$. ### **Phase 1 — Depolarization (Rising Phase)** - **Why:** A stimulus (synaptic input, sensory transducer) opens ligand- or mechanically-gated Na⁺ channels. If depolarization crosses **−55 mV (threshold)**, **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** open en masse. - **How:** $g_{Na}$ increases ~**600-fold** in <1 ms. Na⁺ rushes in down its electrochemical gradient. $V_m$ shoots toward $E_{Na}$ (+60 mV). - **Regenerative feedback:** depolarization → more Na⁺ channels open → more depolarization. ### **Phase 2 — Repolarization (Falling Phase)** - **Why:** Two simultaneous events: 1. **Na⁺ channel inactivation** — the inactivation gate (the "ball-and-chain" h-gate) closes within ~1 ms. 2. **Voltage-gated K⁺ channels (delayed rectifier) open** with a delay. - **How:** $g_{Na}$ collapses; $g_K$ rises. K⁺ efflux drives $V_m$ back toward $E_K$. ### **Phase 3 — Hyperpolarization / Undershoot** - **Why:** Voltage-gated K⁺ channels close slowly, so K⁺ efflux continues briefly, driving $V_m$ momentarily past $V_{rest}$ toward $E_K$ (−90 mV). - **How:** $g_K$ is still elevated. $V_m$ dips to ≈ **−80 mV**. ### **Phase 4 — Return to Rest** - The Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase and leak channels restore ionic gradients. $V_m$ settles at **−70 mV**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Cheat Sheet — Action Potential Equations** | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | **Nernst Equation** — equilibrium potential for a single ion | | 2 | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation** — $V_m$ with multiple permeant ions | | 3 | $I_{ion} = g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$ | **Ohmic (Driving-Force) Current** | | 4 | $C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} = -\sum I_{ion} + I_{stim}$ | **Cable Equation** — membrane capacitance charging | | 5 | $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na}\,m^3 h\,(V_m - E_{Na})$ | **Hodgkin-Huxley Na⁺ Current** (m = activation, h = inactivation) | | 6 | $I_K = \bar{g}_K\,n^4\,(V_m - E_K)$ | **Hodgkin-Huxley K⁺ Current** (n = activation) | | 7 | $\tau \frac{dx}{dt} = x_\infty(V_m) - x$ | **Gating Variable Dynamics** (m, h, n) | | 8 | $v = \sqrt{\frac{d}{2 R_m C_m^2}}$ | **Conduction Velocity (unmyelinated, cable)** | | 9 | $v = \frac{1.1\, d}{\sqrt{R_i R_m}} \times 10^3$ (approx) | **Myelinated Conduction** — proportional to axon diameter | | 10 | $\Delta V = V_o \, e^{-x/\lambda}$, where $\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{R_m d}{4 R_i}}$ | **Passive Decay (Length Constant)** | > **Constants (BOLD, memorise):** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $T = 310$ K (at 37°C), $RT/F \approx 26.7$ mV at body temperature. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering / Real-World Utility:** > The Hodgkin-Huxley model is the conceptual ancestor of modern **neuromorphic engineering** — Intel's *Loihi* and IBM's *TrueNorth* chips encode the same conductance-based dynamics in silicon to mimic spiking neural networks for low-power AI inference. Biologically accurate AP waveforms are also used in **cardiac defibrillation modeling**, **deep brain stimulation design**, and **EEG/ECG signal processing**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation (Single-Ion Equilibrium) The Nernst equation is the **thermodynamic cornerstone** of every AP calculation. The KTU 2024 examiner expects a clean, step-by-step derivation. **Starting Principle:** At electrochemical equilibrium, the net flux of an ion across the membrane is zero. The electrical work done moving a mole of ions against the potential gradient exactly balances the chemical work of the concentration gradient. **Step 1 — Electrical work** to move 1 mole of a $z$-valent ion through a membrane potential difference $\Delta V$: $$W_{elec} = z F \Delta \psi$$ **Step 2 — Chemical work** (Gibbs free energy) of moving 1 mole from concentration $C_1$ to $C_2$: $$W_{chem} = RT \ln \frac{C_1}{C_2}$$ **Step 3 — At equilibrium** $\Delta G_{total} = 0$, so $W_{elec} + W_{chem} = 0$: $$z F \Delta \psi + RT \ln \frac{C_1}{C_2} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange** to isolate the equilibrium potential $E_{ion} = \Delta \psi$ (with outside = o, inside = i): $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{C_o}{C_i}$$ Substituting the **natural logarithm to base-10** conversion $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$, and using the **mV-friendly form** (multiply numerator and denominator by $10^3$ to convert volts to mV): $$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$$ At $T = 310$ K, the constant $\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \approx 61.5$ mV, so: $$E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i} \quad \text{(mV)}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical check** for K⁺ with $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 150$ mM, $z = +1$: $$E_K = \frac{61.5}{1} \log_{10} \frac{5}{150} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.0333)$$ $$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.477)$$ $$E_K \approx -90.8 \text{ mV}$$ This matches the standard KTU value of $E_K \approx -90$ mV. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation The Nernst equation assumes **only one** permeant ion. Real neurons at rest are permeable to K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻ simultaneously. **Derrick Goldman (1943)** derived the multi-ion potential. **Step 1 — Start with the Nernst-Planck constant-field assumption:** the electric field across the membrane is constant (linear). **Step 2 — Integrate the flux for each ion** through the constant field, summing contributions: $$I_{ion} = P_{ion} \, z^2 F^2 \frac{V_m}{RT} \frac{[ion]_i - [ion]_o \, e^{-zF V_m / RT}}{1 - e^{-zF V_m / RT}}$$ **Step 3 — At steady state** ($I_{net} = 0$ on a resting membrane), set $I_K + I_{Na} + I_{Cl} = 0$. **Step 4 — Solve for $V_m$** in terms of permeabilities $P_{ion}$ and concentrations: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Interpretive Point for KTU Exams:** > - For a *resting* neuron, $P_K \gg P_{Na} \gg P_{Cl}$, so $V_m \approx E_K$. > - During the *peak of AP*, $P_{Na}$ momentarily dominates, so $V_m \to E_{Na}$. > - This dynamic change in relative permeabilities is the **physical substrate of the action potential**. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cable Equation & Length Constant $\lambda$ For an **unmyelinated axon** modeled as a cylindrical cable of diameter $d$, membrane resistance $R_m$ (per unit length), and axoplasm resistance $R_i$ (per unit length), the passive voltage spread satisfies: $$V(x) = V_0 \, e^{-x/\lambda}$$ **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Axoplasm current** (axial, per unit length): $I_a = -\frac{\pi d^2}{4 R_i} \frac{dV}{dx}$. **Step 2 — Membrane current** (radial, per unit length): $I_m = \frac{\pi d}{R_m} V$. **Step 3 — Conservation:** $\frac{dI_a}{dx} = I_m$, which gives the **cable PDE**: $$\lambda^2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = V \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \sqrt{\frac{R_m \, d}{4 R_i}}$$ **Step 4 — Solution** for an infinite cable with boundary condition $V(0) = V_0$: $$V(x) = V_0 \, e^{-x/\lambda}$$ **Step 5 — Physical meaning:** $\lambda$ is the **distance over which a passive signal decays to $1/e \approx 37\%$** of its original amplitude. For a typical unmyelinated axon, $\lambda \approx 1$ mm; for myelinated axons, $\lambda$ can reach **1 cm or more** because the myelin sheath dramatically increases $R_m$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why the AP is All-or-None | Stimulus | Outcome | $g_{Na}/g_K$ at peak | |---|---|---| | Sub-threshold (e.g., $V_m = -65$ mV) | Decaying graded potential | $g_{Na} < g_K$ | | Just at threshold (−55 mV) | AP barely fires | $g_{Na} \approx g_K$ | | Supra-threshold (e.g., $V_m = -40$ mV) | Full-amplitude AP | $g_{Na} \gg g_K$ | | Massive supra-threshold (e.g., $V_m = 0$ mV) | **Same** full-amplitude AP | $g_{Na} \gg g_K$ | This tabular matrix illustrates the **All-or-None Principle**: once threshold is crossed, AP amplitude is *independent* of stimulus strength. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Voltage-Gated Channel State Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Closed_Resting Closed_Resting : Na+ channel CLOSED at resting V_m = -70 mV
m-gate closed, h-gate open Closed_Resting --> Open_Activated : Depolarization crosses threshold -55 mV
m-gate swings open rapidly (1 ms) Open_Activated : Na+ channel OPEN
Na+ rushes in, V_m shoots toward +30 mV Open_Activated --> Inactivated : Within ~1 ms
h-gate (inactivation ball) closes Inactivated : Na+ channel INACTIVATED
Cannot reopen until V_m repolarizes Inactivated --> Closed_Resting : Repolarization below -50 mV
h-gate resets, m-gate closes note right of Open_Activated : This is the ONLY state
that conducts Na+ note right of Inactivated : ABSOLUTE REFRACTORY PERIOD
No new AP possible ``` ## 4.2 K+ Channel (Delayed Rectifier) Activation Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Resting V_m = -70 mV] -->|Depolarization to -55 mV| B[n-gates begin to open - SLOW] B -->|Delay ~1 ms| C[K+ channel OPEN, 4 n-gates all open] C -->|V_m near peak +30 mV| D[Peak K+ efflux - Repolarization begins] D -->|V_m returns to -70 mV| E[n-gates close slowly - HYPERPOLARIZATION] E -->|Return to rest| A style A fill:#e0e0e0 style C fill:#ffe0b3 style D fill:#b3d9ff style E fill:#d9b3ff ``` ## 4.3 Full Action Potential Block-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STIMULUS A1[Stimulus arrives - synaptic input or sensory] end subgraph PHASE0[Phase 0 - RESTING] A2[V_m = -70 mV
K+ leak dominates
Na+/K+ pump active] end subgraph PHASE1[Phase 1 - DEPOLARIZATION] A3[Threshold -55 mV crossed
Voltage-gated Na+ opens
V_m rises to +30 mV] end subgraph PHASE2[Phase 2 - REPOLARIZATION] A4[Na+ inactivates
Delayed K+ opens
V_m falls to -70 mV] end subgraph PHASE3[Phase 3 - HYPERPOLARIZATION] A5[K+ still open briefly
V_m dips to -80 mV] end subgraph PHASE4[Phase 4 - RECOVERY] A6[Na+/K+ pump restores gradients
Absolute + Relative refractory period] end A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A4 --> A5 A5 --> A6 A6 --> A2 style A3 fill:#ff9999 style A4 fill:#99ccff style A5 fill:#cc99ff ``` ## 4.4 Refractory Periods Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ABS[ABSOLUTE REFRACTORY PERIOD - ~1 to 2 ms] A1[Na+ channels INACTIVATED
No stimulus, no matter how strong, can evoke AP] end subgraph REL[RELATIVE REFRACTORY PERIOD - ~3 to 4 ms] A2[Na+ channels RECOVERED but K+ still elevated
Stronger-than-normal stimulus CAN evoke AP
Smaller amplitude possible] end A1 -->|Time progression| A2 A2 -->|Return to baseline| A3[Full excitability restored] style ABS fill:#ff6666,color:#fff style REL fill:#ffcc66 style A3 fill:#99ff99 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define action potential. State the all-or-none principle.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **action potential** is a rapid, transient, regenerative change in the membrane potential of an excitable cell, in which the cell interior briefly becomes positive (~+30 mV) relative to the exterior due to a sudden influx of Na⁺ ions through voltage-gated sodium channels, followed by repolarization via K⁺ efflux (3 marks for definition + 2 marks for phases diagram, but here 3 marks concise). **All-or-none principle:** Once the membrane potential crosses the **threshold (~ −55 mV)**, the action potential fires with a fixed, stereotyped amplitude (~100 mV) and is propagated along the entire axon without decrement. If threshold is not reached, no action potential occurs at all — a sub-threshold stimulus merely produces a decaying graded potential. The amplitude of the AP is **independent of stimulus strength** once threshold is crossed (2 marks). ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between absolute and relative refractory periods.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Absolute Refractory Period | Relative Refractory Period | |---|---|---| | **Cause** | Na⁺ channels inactivated (h-gate closed) | Na⁺ channels recovered, K⁺ still elevated | | **AP possible?** | **No**, even with infinite stimulus | Yes, but requires **supra-normal** stimulus | | **AP amplitude** | Zero | Reduced | | **Duration** | ~1–2 ms | ~3–4 ms | | **Direction** | Unidirectional propagation enforced | — | (1 mark per differentiating row × 3 key rows = 3 marks) --- ## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)** ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]** **(a) Describe in detail the ionic basis of an action potential, including the roles of voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels, with reference to the four phases.** (7 marks) **(b) Derive the Nernst equation and calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ given $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 150$ mM at 37°C.** (7 marks) ### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Phase 0 — Resting State (1 mark):** At rest, $V_m = -70$ mV. The membrane is highly permeable to K⁺ due to leak channels. The Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase maintains concentration gradients: high K⁺ inside, high Na⁺ outside. **Phase 1 — Depolarization (2 marks):** A stimulus depolarizes the membrane to **threshold (−55 mV)**. Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels** open rapidly (m-gate swings open within 0.1–0.3 ms). Na⁺ flows in down its electrochemical gradient (inside-negative, high-outside Na⁺), driving $V_m$ toward $E_{Na} = +60$ mV. This is **regenerative**: depolarization opens more Na⁺ channels. Peak $V_m \approx +30$ to $+40$ mV. **Phase 2 — Repolarization (2 marks):** Two events: (i) Na⁺ channels **inactivate** (h-gate closes after ~1 ms), and (ii) voltage-gated **K⁺ channels (delayed rectifier)** open with a delay of ~1 ms. The 4 n-subunits swing open cooperatively. K⁺ efflux drives $V_m$ back toward $E_K = -90$ mV. **Phase 3 — Hyperpolarization & Recovery (2 marks):** K⁺ channels close slowly, causing a brief **after-hyperpolarization** to ≈ −80 mV. Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase restores ionic gradients. The cell enters the **refractory periods** before returning to full excitability. ### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Derivation of Nernst Equation (4 marks):** At electrochemical equilibrium, the electrical work $W_{elec} = zFV$ moving 1 mole of ion through potential $V$ equals the negative of the chemical work $W_{chem} = RT \ln \frac{C_o}{C_i}$: $$zFV + RT \ln \frac{C_o}{C_i} = 0$$ Solving for $V$: $$E_{ion} = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{C_o}{C_i} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{C_i}{C_o}$$ Converting to $\log_{10}$ and using $T = 310$ K: $$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_i}{[ion]_o} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[ion]_i}{[ion]_o} \text{ mV}$$ **Numerical Calculation for K⁺ (3 marks):** Given $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 150$ mM, $z = +1$: $$E_K = \frac{61.5}{1} \log_{10} \frac{150}{5} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(30)$$ $$E_K = 61.5 \times 1.477 = 90.8 \text{ mV (interior)}$$ Therefore $E_K = -90.8$ mV (with sign convention: inside relative to outside). **This matches the canonical KTU textbook value of $E_K \approx -90$ mV.** ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]** **(a) Explain the Hodgkin-Huxley model of the action potential. Define the gating variables m, h, and n and their functional roles.** (7 marks) **(b) Explain saltatory conduction in myelinated axons. Why is it faster than continuous conduction in unmyelinated axons?** (7 marks) ### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** The **Hodgkin-Huxley model (1952)** — for which Alan Hodgkin and Andrew Huxley received the **1963 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine** — describes the AP in terms of voltage- and time-dependent ionic conductances. The total membrane current is the sum of capacitive, Na⁺, K⁺, and leak currents: $$I_m = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) + \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L)$$ **Gating variables (3 marks):** - **m** — Na⁺ **activation** variable. Rises rapidly (τ_m ~ 0.1 ms) upon depolarization. Raised to the **third power** because the Na⁺ channel has 3 independent m-subunits that must all be open. - **h** — Na⁺ **inactivation** variable.
